question: What is the most important reason for doing a vehicle inspection?
options: To keep the logbook updated, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
To ensure the vehicle looks good, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
To check the fuel level, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Safety is the most important reason you inspect your vehicle
correct_answer: Safety is the most important reason you inspect your vehicle
explanation: Safety is the most important reason you inspect your vehicle, safety for yourself and for other road users.

question: What things should you check during a trip?
options: Use your senses to check for problems (look, listen, smell, feel)
Check critical items when you stop (tires, brakes, lights)
All of the above
Watch gauges for signs of trouble
correct_answer: All of the above
explanation: You should watch gauges, use your senses, and check critical items like tires, wheels, brakes, lights, and electrical connections when you stop.

question: Name some key steering system parts.
options: Steering arm, spindle, steering knuckle
Drag link, pitman arm, gear box
All of the above
Steering wheel, tie rod, steering shaft
correct_answer: All of the above
explanation: Key steering system parts include the steering wheel, tie rod, steering shaft, steering arm, spindle, steering knuckle, drag link, pitman arm, and gear box.

question: What is the minimum tread depth for front tires?
options: 1/32-inch
6/32-inch
4/32-inch
2/32-inch
correct_answer: 4/32-inch
explanation: You need at least 4/32-inch tread depth in every major groove on front tires. You need 2/32-inch on other tires.

question: Which of these is NOT a sign of wheel or rim problems?
options: Tires that are the same size
Welding repairs on the rim
Missing clamps, spacers, studs, or lugs
Rust around wheel nuts
correct_answer: Tires that are the same size
explanation: Rust around wheel nuts may mean the nuts are loose. Missing parts and welding repairs are dangerous. Tires of the same size are good.

question: How do you test hydraulic brakes for leaks?
options: Look for puddles under the truck, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Drive at 5 mph and slam on the brakes, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Listen for air escaping, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Pump the brake pedal 3 times, then apply firm pressure for 5 seconds
correct_answer: Pump the brake pedal 3 times, then apply firm pressure for 5 seconds
explanation: Pump the brake pedal three times. Then apply firm pressure to the pedal and hold for five seconds. The pedal should not move. If it does, there may be a leak.

question: Why should you back toward the driver's side?
options: So you can see better
It is required by law
The mirrors are bigger on the left
It is always safer to back to the right
correct_answer: So you can see better
explanation: Back toward the driver's side so you can see better. Backing toward the right side is very dangerous because you can't see as well.

question: What is a 'pull-up'?
options: Pulling over to the side of the road, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Doing exercise during a break, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Pulling a trailer up a hill, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Pulling forward to reposition the vehicle when backing
correct_answer: Pulling forward to reposition the vehicle when backing
explanation: When backing a truck, if you need to correct your position, you pull forward. This is called a pull-up.

question: How far ahead does the manual say you should look?
options: Just past the hood
5 to 10 seconds
12 to 15 seconds
20 to 30 seconds
correct_answer: 12 to 15 seconds
explanation: Most good drivers look at least 12 to 15 seconds ahead. That means looking for what is going to happen in the distance, not just right in front of you.

question: What are two main things to look for ahead?
options: Billboards and scenery
Traffic and road conditions
Rest stops and food
Police and weigh stations
correct_answer: Traffic and road conditions
explanation: Look for traffic (vehicles coming onto the highway, into your lane, or turning) and road conditions (hills, curves, traffic signals).

question: What is the most important hand signal that you and the helper should agree on?
options: Right
Stop
Go
Left
correct_answer: Stop
explanation: The most important hand signal to agree on is 'Stop'. If you cannot see the helper, you should stop immediately.

question: Which of these statements about backing a heavy vehicle is true?
options: You don't need to check behind you if you have mirrors
You should avoid backing whenever you can
It is safer to back toward the right side of the vehicle
Helpers should stand directly behind the vehicle
correct_answer: You should avoid backing whenever you can
explanation: Backing is always dangerous. You should avoid backing whenever you can. When you park, try to park so you will be able to pull forward when you leave.

question: What is the proper way to hold a steering wheel?
options: With one hand at the bottom, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
With your arms crossed, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
With one hand at the top, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Firmly with both hands on opposite sides of the wheel
correct_answer: Firmly with both hands on opposite sides of the wheel
explanation: Hold the wheel firmly with both hands. Your hands should be on opposite sides of the wheel. If you hit a curb or a pothole (chuckhole), the wheel could pull away from your hands unless you have a firm hold.

question: How does the weight of the vehicle affect stopping distance?
options: Heavier trucks stop faster because of traction
Weight does not affect stopping distance
Heavier trucks take longer to stop
Empty trucks take longer to stop than loaded trucks
correct_answer: Heavier trucks take longer to stop
explanation: The heavier the vehicle, the more work the brakes must do to stop it, and the more heat they absorb. However, empty trucks can take longer to stop than loaded ones if they have less traction, but generally, more weight means more stopping distance.

question: When you are driving on a downgrade, you should:
options: Use only the service brakes
Coast in neutral
Shift to a higher gear
Shift to a lower gear
correct_answer: Shift to a lower gear
explanation: You should shift to a lower gear before starting down a grade. This allows the engine compression to help slow the vehicle.

question: What is the 'reaction distance'?
options: The distance you can see ahead, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
The distance you travel while your brain tells your foot to hit the brake
The distance you travel after you hit the brake, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
The time it takes to see a hazard, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: The distance you travel while your brain tells your foot to hit the brake
explanation: Reaction distance is the distance your vehicle travels while you are reacting to a hazard. The average driver has a reaction time of 3/4 second.

question: What happens when you double your speed?
options: Your stopping distance stays the same
Your stopping distance becomes 4 times longer
Your stopping distance becomes 3 times longer
Your stopping distance doubles, only after completing a thorough pre-trip safety inspection
correct_answer: Your stopping distance becomes 4 times longer
explanation: When you double your speed, the impact is 4 times greater. The braking distance is also 4 times longer.

question: Which of these is a good rule for using turn signals?
options: Signal early
All of the above
Cancel your signal after the turn
Signal continuously
correct_answer: All of the above
explanation: You should signal well before you turn. Signal continuously until you have completed the turn. Cancel your signal after you have turned so others don't think you are turning again.

question: Where should you place your warning devices if you must stop on a one-way or divided highway?
options: 10 feet, 100 feet, and 200 feet toward the approaching traffic
100 feet, 200 feet, and 300 feet toward the approaching traffic
Anywhere visible, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
10 feet front, 10 feet rear, 100 feet rear, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: 10 feet, 100 feet, and 200 feet toward the approaching traffic
explanation: If you stop on a one-way or divided highway, place warning devices 10 feet, 100 feet, and 200 feet toward the approaching traffic.

question: What is the first thing you should do if your brakes fail while going down a hill?
options: Call for help, as long as all safety systems have been tested and verified
Look for an escape ramp
Turn off the engine
Try to use the parking brake
correct_answer: Look for an escape ramp
explanation: If your brakes fail on a downgrade, look for an escape ramp. They are designed to stop runaway vehicles safely.

question: What is 'hydroplaning'?
options: Skidding on ice, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Washing your truck, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Driving through deep water, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
When tires lose contact with the road and ride on water
correct_answer: When tires lose contact with the road and ride on water
explanation: Hydroplaning is like water skiing; the tires lose their contact with the road and have little or no traction. You may not be able to steer or brake.

question: Which of these is true about radiator shutters and the winterfront?
options: The winterfront should be closed tightly
They help keep the engine cool, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
You should remove ice from the radiator shutters
They are only for decoration, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: You should remove ice from the radiator shutters
explanation: Remove any ice from the radiator shutters. Make sure the winterfront is not closed too tightly. If the shutters freeze shut or the winterfront is closed too much, the engine may overheat and stop.

question: What is the best advice for driving in fog?
options: Use high beams, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Drive faster to get out of it, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Follow close to other vehicles
Don't drive in fog if you can avoid it
correct_answer: Don't drive in fog if you can avoid it
explanation: The best advice for driving in fog is: Don't. It is preferable that you pull off the road into a rest area or truck stop until visibility is better.

question: What should you do if you are being tailgated?
options: Tap your brakes, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Signal the driver to pass
Increase your following distance
Speed up, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: Increase your following distance
explanation: If you are being tailgated, you should increase your following distance. This makes it easier for the tailgater to pass you and safer for you if you have to stop.

question: How long does it take for a typical tractor-trailer to clear a double track?
options: 20 seconds
10 seconds
14 seconds
15 seconds
correct_answer: 15 seconds
explanation: It takes a typical tractor-trailer unit at least 14 seconds to clear a single track and more than 15 seconds to clear a double track.

question: Which of these is a danger of crossing railroad tracks with a steep grade?
options: Going too fast, once the vehicle has been inspected and cleared for operation
Losing your load, according to the federal motor carrier safety regulations
Damaging the tires, unless the vehicle is equipped with an automatic safety override
Getting stuck on the tracks
correct_answer: Getting stuck on the tracks
explanation: Low slung units (lowboy, car carrier, moving van, possum-belly livestock trailer) can get stuck on raised crossings.

question: What is 'brake fade'?
options: When the air pressure is low, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
When brakes get old, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
When brakes overheat and lose stopping power
When the brake pedal feels soft, provided all air pressure gauges read within normal operating range
correct_answer: When brakes overheat and lose stopping power
explanation: Brakes can fade or fail from excessive heat caused by using them too much and not relying on the engine braking effect.

question: The proper way to go down long, steep grades is to:
options: Use the snub braking technique
Use the brakes firmly, after verifying all brake components are properly adjusted
Coast in neutral, only after completing a thorough pre-trip safety inspection
Use a low gear and light, steady pressure on the brakes
correct_answer: Use the snub braking technique
explanation: The proper braking technique is to select a gear that will keep your vehicle at a safe speed, then use a light, steady pressure (snub braking) to keep the speed from increasing.

question: What is the 'snub braking' technique?
options: Holding the brakes down constantly, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Applying the brakes until you slow down 5 mph below your safe speed, then releasing
Applying the brakes hard for 10 seconds, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Pumping the brakes rapidly, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: Applying the brakes until you slow down 5 mph below your safe speed, then releasing
explanation: Apply the brakes just hard enough to feel a definite slowdown. When your speed has been reduced to approximately 5 mph below your 'safe' speed, release the brakes.

question: What is the definition of a 'hazard'?
options: Any road condition or other road user that is a possible danger
A bad driver, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
A construction zone, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
A traffic light, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: Any road condition or other road user that is a possible danger
explanation: A hazard is any road condition or other road user (driver, bicyclist, pedestrian) that is a possible danger.

question: Why are placards used?
options: To make the truck look nice, unless directed otherwise by law enforcement or a flag person
To show the company name, when hauling hazardous materials over a designated route
To warn others of hazardous materials
To show the weight of the truck
correct_answer: To warn others of hazardous materials
explanation: Placards are used to warn others of hazardous materials. They are diamond-shaped signs placed on the outside of the vehicle.

question: If you are driving a vehicle with a hazardous material placard, where must the shipping papers be?
options: In the glove box, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
In your pocket, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
In the trailer, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
In a pouch on the driver's door or in clear view within reach
correct_answer: In a pouch on the driver's door or in clear view within reach
explanation: Shipping papers must be within reach of the driver while driving (e.g., in a pouch on the driver's door) or on the driver's seat when out of the vehicle.

question: You should check your mirrors:
options: Only when changing lanes
When you hear a horn
Regularly
Every 4 seconds
correct_answer: Regularly
explanation: Check your mirrors regularly. You should know what is going on around your vehicle.

question: Convex mirrors (curved mirrors):
options: Make things look closer than they are
Are not useful, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Are illegal, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Show a wider area than flat mirrors
correct_answer: Show a wider area than flat mirrors
explanation: Convex mirrors show a wider area than flat mirrors. They are often used to see the 'blind spot'.

question: When you are parked at the side of the road at night, you must:
options: Turn on your 4-way emergency flashers
Use your high beams, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Put out flares immediately, as long as all safety systems have been tested and verified
Turn off all lights, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: Turn on your 4-way emergency flashers
explanation: Turn on your 4-way emergency flashers. This is important at night. Drivers have crashed into the rear of a parked vehicle because they thought it was moving.

question: Which of these is true about tire pressure?
options: You should let air out of hot tires
Air pressure decreases with temperature
Air pressure increases with temperature
You should check pressure when tires are hot
correct_answer: Air pressure increases with temperature
explanation: Air pressure increases with temperature. Do not let air out or the pressure will be too low when the tires cool off.

question: What is the 'blind spot'?
options: The area behind the truck, when hauling hazardous materials over a designated route
The area under the truck, when hauling hazardous materials over a designated route
The area directly in front of the truck
The area you cannot see in your mirrors
correct_answer: The area you cannot see in your mirrors
explanation: Blind spots are areas you cannot see in your mirrors. You should know where they are and check them before changing lanes.

question: If you need to leave the road in a traffic emergency, you should:
options: Brake hard, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Turn sharply, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Avoid braking until your speed has dropped to about 20 mph
Honk your horn, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: Avoid braking until your speed has dropped to about 20 mph
explanation: Avoid braking until your speed has dropped to about 20 mph. Then brake gently to avoid skidding on a loose surface.

question: What is 'controlled braking'?
options: Using the parking brake, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Using the engine brake, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Pumping the brakes, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Applying the brakes as hard as you can without locking the wheels
correct_answer: Applying the brakes as hard as you can without locking the wheels
explanation: Controlled braking is applying the brakes as hard as you can without locking the wheels. Keep steering wheel movements very small.

question: What is 'stab braking'?
options: Light steady pressure, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Applying the brakes all the way, releasing when wheels lock, then reapplying
Using the hand brake, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Braking only in emergencies, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: Applying the brakes all the way, releasing when wheels lock, then reapplying
explanation: Stab braking involves applying your brakes all the way, releasing brakes when wheels lock up, and as soon as the wheels start rolling, apply the brakes fully again.

question: How do you correct a rear-wheel acceleration skid?
options: Apply the brakes
Stop accelerating
Shift to a lower gear
Steer in the opposite direction
correct_answer: Stop accelerating
explanation: Stop accelerating. The engine is providing too much power to the wheels. Taking your foot off the accelerator will stop the skid.

question: What should you do if your vehicle catches fire?
options: Use water on a gasoline fire, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Park in an open area, away from buildings and trees
Open the hood to see the fire, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Drive to a service station, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: Park in an open area, away from buildings and trees
explanation: Park in an open area, away from buildings, trees, and other vehicles. Don't pull into a service station!

question: Which class of fire extinguisher is used for electrical fires?
options: Class B
Class C
Class A
Class D
correct_answer: Class C
explanation: Class C extinguishers are for electrical fires. Class B is for flammable liquids.

question: What is the legal limit for blood alcohol concentration (BAC) for commercial drivers?
explanation: The legal limit for BAC for commercial drivers is 0.04%. If you are found to have a BAC of 0.04% or more, you will lose your CDL for at least one year.

question: What happens if you refuse to take an alcohol test?
options: You will be fined, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
You will be given a warning, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Nothing, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
You will lose your CDL for at least one year
correct_answer: You will lose your CDL for at least one year
explanation: If you refuse to take an alcohol test, you will lose your CDL for at least one year. It is treated the same as driving with a BAC of 0.04%.

question: What is the purpose of the 'pre-trip inspection'?
options: To find problems that could cause a crash or breakdown
To clean the truck, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
To check the radio, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
To waste time, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: To find problems that could cause a crash or breakdown
explanation: The purpose of the pre-trip inspection is to find problems that could cause a crash or breakdown. It is a safety requirement.

question: Which of these should you check for during the 'in-cab' inspection?
options: Voltmeter
Oil pressure gauge
All of the above
Air pressure gauge
correct_answer: All of the above
explanation: You should check all gauges, including oil pressure, voltmeter, and air pressure, to ensure they are working correctly.

question: What is the 'emergency air line'?
options: The red line
The green line
The blue line
The black line
correct_answer: The red line
explanation: The emergency air line is usually red. It supplies air to the trailer air tanks and controls the emergency brakes.

question: What is the 'service air line'?
options: The black line
The blue line
The red line
The green line
correct_answer: The blue line
explanation: The service air line is usually blue. It carries air to the relay valve that allows the trailer brakes to be applied when you press the brake pedal.

question: What should you do if you are sleepy while driving?
options: Stop and sleep
Turn up the radio
Open the window
Drink coffee
correct_answer: Stop and sleep
explanation: The only cure for sleepiness is sleep. If you are sleepy, stop at a safe place and get some sleep.

question: What is the gross vehicle weight rating (GVWR)?
options: The maximum weight specified by the manufacturer for a single vehicle plus its load
The weight of the load only, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
The weight of the vehicle plus the trailer, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
The weight of the vehicle when empty, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: The maximum weight specified by the manufacturer for a single vehicle plus its load
explanation: GVWR is the value specified by the manufacturer as the loaded weight of a single vehicle.

question: What is the gross combination weight rating (GCWR)?
options: The weight of the power unit, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures, after verifying all brake components are properly adjusted
The maximum weight specified by the manufacturer for a combination of vehicles plus its load
The weight of the fuel, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
The weight of the trailer, after confirming all safety checks have been completed, following established DOT pre-trip inspection requirements
correct_answer: The maximum weight specified by the manufacturer for a combination of vehicles plus its load
explanation: GCWR is the value specified by the manufacturer as the loaded weight of a combination (articulated) vehicle.

question: What happens if you have too little weight on the steering axle?
options: The steering axle can rise off the road
Poor traction
All of the above
Hard steering
correct_answer: All of the above
explanation: Too little weight on the steering axle can cause hard steering and poor traction. The front wheels may even lose contact with the road.

question: How many tie-downs are required for a 20-foot load?
explanation: You should have one tie-down for every 10 feet of cargo. A 20-foot load requires at least 2 tie-downs.

question: What is the minimum number of tie-downs for any flatbed load?
options: Depends on the weight
explanation: No matter how small the cargo, it should have at least two tie-downs.

question: Which of these is a rule for transporting sealed loads?
options: You must open the seal to check the load, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
You cannot inspect sealed loads, but you should check that the seal is intact
You are not responsible for sealed loads, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Sealed loads do not need to be secured, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: You cannot inspect sealed loads, but you should check that the seal is intact
explanation: You cannot inspect sealed loads, but you should check that the seal is intact and that the load is properly secured if possible (e.g., check gross weight).

question: What is the 'center of gravity'?
options: The middle of the truck, only when driving under a special state or federal permit
The engine, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
The point where the weight is balanced
The heaviest part of the load, according to the federal motor carrier safety regulations
correct_answer: The point where the weight is balanced
explanation: The center of gravity is the point where the weight of an object is balanced. High center of gravity makes a vehicle more likely to tip over.

question: If you are carrying a heavy load that is slowing you down, you should:
options: Honk at people to move
Drive on the shoulder
Drive in the left lane
Use your 4-way flashers
correct_answer: Use your 4-way flashers
explanation: If you are driving slowly due to a heavy load, use your 4-way emergency flashers to warn other drivers.

question: What is 'blocking and bracing'?
options: A way to park, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
A way to prevent cargo from sliding
A type of brake, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
A type of steering, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: A way to prevent cargo from sliding
explanation: Blocking is used in the front, back, and/or sides of a piece of cargo to keep it from sliding. Bracing goes from the upper part of the cargo to the floor and/or walls of the cargo compartment.

question: Which of these loads needs special attention?
options: All of the above
Dry bulk
Hanging meat
Livestock
correct_answer: All of the above
explanation: Dry bulk tanks can have a high center of gravity. Hanging meat can be unstable. Livestock can move around.

question: What is the most common cause of serious vehicle skids?
options: Poorly loaded cargo, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Driving too fast for road conditions
Bad tires, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Bad brakes, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: Driving too fast for road conditions
explanation: Driving too fast for road conditions causes most serious skids. Drivers who adjust their driving to conditions don't over-accelerate and don't have to over-brake or over-steer.

question: When should you downshift automatic transmissions?
options: When stopping, as long as all safety systems have been tested and verified
When going up a hill
Never, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
When going down a hill
correct_answer: When going down a hill
explanation: You should downshift automatic transmissions when going down a long or steep downgrade to use the braking effect of the engine.

question: Retarders keep you from skidding when the road is slippery. True or False?
options: False
True
correct_answer: False
explanation: False. Retarders can cause the drive wheels to skid when the road is slippery. You should turn the retarder off when the road is wet, icy, or snow-covered.

question: How far ahead should you look while driving?
options: 1-2 seconds
12-15 seconds
5-10 seconds
As far as you can see
correct_answer: 12-15 seconds
explanation: You should look at least 12 to 15 seconds ahead. At lower speeds, that's about one block. At highway speeds, it's about a quarter of a mile.

question: What are the two types of mirrors?
options: Large and Small
Glass and Plastic
Flat and Convex
Round and Square
correct_answer: Flat and Convex
explanation: The two types of mirrors are flat mirrors and convex (curved) mirrors.

question: How do you check your blind spots?
options: Use your mirrors
Guess, as specified in the state commercial driver licensing manual
Turn your head
Ask a passenger
correct_answer: Turn your head
explanation: You must turn your head to check your blind spots that mirrors cannot see, or use convex mirrors if equipped.

question: What is the 'three-second rule'?
options: A rule for signaling, unless the vehicle is equipped with an automatic safety override
A rule for checking mirrors
A rule for stopping, as specified in the state commercial driver licensing manual
A rule for following distance
correct_answer: A rule for following distance
explanation: The three-second rule is a way to determine a safe following distance. (Note: For heavy trucks, it's often 1 second for every 10 feet of vehicle length, plus 1 second if going over 40 mph, which usually adds up to more than 3 seconds, but the concept is similar).

question: If you are driving a 40-foot vehicle at 35 mph, how many seconds of following distance should you leave?
options: 3 seconds
4 seconds
5 seconds
2 seconds
correct_answer: 4 seconds
explanation: The formula is 1 second for every 10 feet of vehicle length. 40 feet = 4 seconds. Since you are going under 40 mph, you don't add the extra second.

question: If you are driving a 40-foot vehicle at 50 mph, how many seconds of following distance should you leave?
options: 6 seconds
4 seconds
3 seconds
5 seconds
correct_answer: 5 seconds
explanation: 40 feet = 4 seconds. Since you are going over 40 mph, add 1 second. Total = 5 seconds.

question: What should you do if you miss your exit on a highway?
options: Make a U-turn
Go to the next exit
Back up on the shoulder
Stop and wait
correct_answer: Go to the next exit
explanation: If you miss your exit, go to the next one. Never back up on a highway.

question: What is the danger of 'distracted driving'?
options: All of the above
It causes accidents
It is annoying
It is illegal
correct_answer: All of the above
explanation: Distracted driving is a major cause of accidents and is illegal in many places.

question: Which of these is a distraction?
options: Texting, after verifying all brake components are properly adjusted
All of the above
Eating, only when driving under a special state or federal permit
Talking on the phone
correct_answer: All of the above
explanation: Anything that takes your attention away from driving is a distraction.

question: What is 'aggressive driving'?
options: Driving in a bold or pushy manner
Driving at night, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Driving fast, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Driving a red truck, after verifying all brake components are properly adjusted
correct_answer: Driving in a bold or pushy manner
explanation: Aggressive driving is the act of operating a motor vehicle in a selfish, bold, or pushy manner, without regard for the rights or safety of others.

question: What is 'road rage'?
options: Driving fast, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Operating a vehicle with the intent of doing harm to others
Being angry at the road, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Honking the horn, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: Operating a vehicle with the intent of doing harm to others
explanation: Road rage is operating a motor vehicle with the intent of doing harm to others or physically assaulting a driver or their vehicle.

question: What should you do if confronted by an aggressive driver?
options: Honk back, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Get out of the car, provided all air pressure gauges read within normal operating range
Ignore them and avoid eye contact
Challenge them, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: Ignore them and avoid eye contact
explanation: Make every attempt to get out of their way. Do not challenge them. Avoid eye contact. Ignore gestures.

question: When driving at night, you should use your high beams when:
options: There are oncoming vehicles
You are following another vehicle
It is safe and legal to do so
It is foggy, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: It is safe and legal to do so
explanation: Use high beams when it is safe and legal to do so. Dim them when within 500 feet of an oncoming vehicle or following another vehicle.

question: If an oncoming driver does not dim their high beams, you should:
options: Look to the right side of the road
Flash your high beams, after verifying all brake components are properly adjusted
Close your eyes, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Turn on your high beams, provided the load is properly secured and within legal limits
correct_answer: Look to the right side of the road
explanation: Look to the right side of the road (the white line) to avoid being blinded.

question: What is the best way to keep from getting tired on a long trip?
options: Schedule trips for the hours you are normally awake
Drink caffeine, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Listen to loud music, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Drive faster, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: Schedule trips for the hours you are normally awake
explanation: Schedule trips for the hours you are normally awake. Your body has a natural clock.

question: How often should you check your tires in hot weather?
options: Once a day, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Every 2 hours or 100 miles
Never, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Every 4 hours, when operating a vehicle with a GVWR over 26,001 pounds
correct_answer: Every 2 hours or 100 miles
explanation: Inspect tires every 2 hours or every 100 miles when driving in very hot weather. Air pressure increases with heat.

question: What should you do if your engine overheats?
options: Open the radiator cap immediately
Stop immediately and let it cool
Pour water on it, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Keep driving, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: Stop immediately and let it cool
explanation: Stop immediately. Never remove the radiator cap until the system has cooled.

question: What is the 'passive' highway-rail crossing?
options: A bridge, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
A crossing with gates, after verifying all brake components are properly adjusted
A crossing without lights or gates
A crossing with lights, when hauling hazardous materials over a designated route
correct_answer: A crossing without lights or gates
explanation: A passive crossing does not have any type of traffic control device. You must stop and look.

question: What is an 'active' highway-rail crossing?
options: A crossing with a traffic control device
A crossing without lights, provided the load is properly secured and within legal limits
A dirt road, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
A tunnel, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: A crossing with a traffic control device
explanation: An active crossing has a traffic control device installed at the crossing to regulate traffic (lights, gates, bells).

question: When approaching a railroad crossing, you should:
options: Slow down and be ready to stop
Speed up to beat the train
Honk your horn, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Change gears on the tracks
correct_answer: Slow down and be ready to stop
explanation: Slow down, look and listen for the train, and be ready to stop.

question: You should never shift gears while crossing railroad tracks. True or False?
options: False
True
correct_answer: True
explanation: True. Do not shift gears while crossing railroad tracks. If you miss a gear, you could get stuck on the tracks.

question: What is the 'move over' law?
options: You must move over for emergency vehicles stopped on the side of the road
You must move to the right lane, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
You must move over for faster traffic, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
You must move to the left lane, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: You must move over for emergency vehicles stopped on the side of the road
explanation: Move Over laws require drivers to slow down and move over to a lane away from emergency vehicles stopped on the side of the road.

question: What is the 'no-zone'?
options: The blind spots around a large vehicle
A parking zone, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
A speed zone, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
A loading zone, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: The blind spots around a large vehicle
explanation: No-Zones are danger areas around trucks and buses where crashes are more likely to occur (blind spots).

question: How does a 'jake brake' (engine brake) work?
options: It uses friction, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
It uses air pressure, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
It uses water, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
It uses engine compression to slow the vehicle
correct_answer: It uses engine compression to slow the vehicle
explanation: An engine brake (retarder) helps slow the vehicle by using the engine's compression, saving wear on the service brakes.

question: When should you NOT use an engine brake?
options: On wet or slippery roads
Going down a hill
In the city, as long as all safety systems have been tested and verified
On dry roads, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
correct_answer: On wet or slippery roads
explanation: Do not use engine brakes on wet, icy, or slippery roads, as they can cause the drive wheels to skid.

question: What is 'off-tracking'?
options: When the truck goes off the road, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
When the GPS is lost, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
When the rear wheels follow a different path than the front wheels
When the load falls off, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: When the rear wheels follow a different path than the front wheels
explanation: Off-tracking (or cheater) is when the rear wheels follow a different path than the front wheels while turning. The rear wheels track closer to the curb.

question: When turning right, you should:
options: Speed up, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Keep the rear of your vehicle close to the curb
Turn from the left lane, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Swing wide to the left, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: Keep the rear of your vehicle close to the curb
explanation: Keep the rear of your vehicle close to the curb. This will stop other drivers from passing you on the right.

question: When turning left, you should:
options: Reach the center of the intersection before turning
Turn early, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Cut the corner, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Turn from the right lane, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: Reach the center of the intersection before turning
explanation: Reach the center of the intersection before you start the left turn. If you turn too soon, the left side of your vehicle may hit another vehicle because of off-tracking.

question: What is a 'jug handle' turn?
options: A parking maneuver
A type of left turn
A U-turn, when hauling hazardous materials over a designated route
A type of right turn
correct_answer: A type of right turn
explanation: Some right turns are so tight you may need to swing left before turning right. This is dangerous. It is better to swing wide as you complete the turn.

question: What is the most common cause of jackknifing?
options: Turning too fast
Overloading
Accelerating too fast
Braking too hard
correct_answer: Braking too hard
explanation: The most common cause of jackknifing is braking too hard. The wheels lock up and the trailer swings around.

question: If you must stop on the shoulder of a road, you should:
options: Turn on your flashers and put out warning devices
Read a map, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Turn off your lights, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Rest in the sleeper berth until the situation resolves itself
correct_answer: Turn on your flashers and put out warning devices
explanation: Turn on your flashers immediately. Put out warning devices within 10 minutes.

question: How do you check for a 'soft' brake pedal?
options: Look at it, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Smell it, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Listen to it, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Press the pedal and see if it goes to the floor
correct_answer: Press the pedal and see if it goes to the floor
explanation: If the brake pedal feels soft or spongy, or goes to the floor, there may be air in the lines or a leak.

question: What is the 'air compressor'?
options: It dries the air, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
It cools the air, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
It pumps air into the air storage tanks
It filters the air, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: It pumps air into the air storage tanks
explanation: The air compressor pumps air into the air storage tanks (reservoirs).

question: What is the 'air compressor governor'?
options: It controls when the air compressor pumps air
It controls the speed of the truck, unless the vehicle is equipped with an automatic safety override
It controls the engine, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
It controls the brakes, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: It controls when the air compressor pumps air
explanation: The governor controls when the air compressor will pump air into the air storage tanks.

question: At what pressure should the air compressor governor cut-out?
options: 150 psi
100 psi
125 psi
85 psi
correct_answer: 125 psi
explanation: The governor should cut-out (stop pumping air) at around 125 psi.

question: At what pressure should the air compressor governor cut-in?
options: 100 psi
125 psi
85 psi
50 psi
correct_answer: 100 psi
explanation: The governor should cut-in (start pumping air) at around 100 psi.

question: What is an 'alcohol evaporator'?
options: It cools the engine, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
It removes alcohol from the driver, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
It puts alcohol into the air system to prevent freezing
It cleans the windshield, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: It puts alcohol into the air system to prevent freezing
explanation: Some air brake systems have an alcohol evaporator to put alcohol into the air system. This helps to reduce the risk of ice in air brake valves and other parts during cold weather.

question: What is the 'safety relief valve'?
options: It protects the air tank from over-pressure
It drains water from the tank, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
It measures air pressure, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
It controls the brakes, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: It protects the air tank from over-pressure
explanation: The safety relief valve protects the tank and the rest of the system from too much pressure. The valve is usually set to open at 150 psi.

question: What is the purpose of the 'brake pedal free play'?
options: To ensure the brakes are not dragging
To test the brakes, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
To allow the brakes to cool, once the vehicle has been inspected and cleared for operation
To adjust the seat, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: To ensure the brakes are not dragging
explanation: Free play in the brake pedal is the distance the pedal moves before the brakes begin to work. Some free play is normal, but too much means the brakes may not work properly.

question: What are the three types of emergency equipment you must have?
options: Spare tire, jack, lug wrench, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Cell phone, GPS, map, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Fire extinguisher, spare fuses, warning devices
First aid kit, flares, radio, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: Fire extinguisher, spare fuses, warning devices
explanation: You must have a fire extinguisher, spare electrical fuses (unless equipped with circuit breakers), and warning devices for parked vehicles.

question: How many reflective triangles must you carry?
explanation: You must have three red reflective triangles to use as warning devices.

question: Within how many minutes must you put out warning devices if you stop on the roadway?
options: 10 minutes
20 minutes
5 minutes
15 minutes
correct_answer: 10 minutes
explanation: You must put out warning devices within 10 minutes of stopping on the roadway.

question: Where should you place warning devices on a two-lane road?
options: Anywhere visible, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
10 feet rear, 100 feet rear, 200 feet rear
50 feet front, 50 feet rear, 100 feet rear
10 feet front, 100 feet rear, 100 feet front
correct_answer: 10 feet rear, 100 feet rear, 200 feet rear
explanation: On a two-lane road, place devices 10 feet, 100 feet, and 200 feet toward approaching traffic (behind your vehicle).

question: If you must stop on or by a one-way or divided highway, where should you place the warning devices?
options: 10 feet, 100 feet, and 200 feet toward approaching traffic
On both sides of the vehicle, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Behind the vehicle only, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
In front of the vehicle only, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: 10 feet, 100 feet, and 200 feet toward approaching traffic
explanation: Place warning devices 10 feet, 100 feet, and 200 feet toward approaching traffic on a one-way or divided highway.

question: If you stop on a curve or hill where drivers cannot see you within 500 feet, where should you place the warning device?
options: Back down the road to give adequate warning
Don't use warning devices, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Right behind your vehicle, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
In front of your vehicle, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: Back down the road to give adequate warning
explanation: Move the rear-most triangle to a point back down the road so drivers will have enough warning to avoid you.

question: What is the maximum hours you can drive in a day under the 11-hour rule?
options: 12 hours
10 hours
14 hours
11 hours
correct_answer: 11 hours
explanation: You may drive a maximum of 11 hours after 10 consecutive hours off duty.

question: What is the 14-hour rule?
options: You cannot drive beyond the 14th hour after coming on duty
You must take a 14-hour break, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
You can drive 14 hours straight, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
You must log 14 hours of work, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: You cannot drive beyond the 14th hour after coming on duty
explanation: You may not drive beyond the 14th consecutive hour after coming on duty, following 10 consecutive hours off duty.

question: What is the 60/70-hour rule?
options: You can drive 70 hours per week, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
You must take a 60-minute break, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
You must rest 60 hours every week, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
You may not drive after 60/70 hours on duty in 7/8 consecutive days
correct_answer: You may not drive after 60/70 hours on duty in 7/8 consecutive days
explanation: You may not drive after being on duty for 60 hours in 7 consecutive days, or 70 hours in 8 consecutive days.

question: What is the 30-minute break rule?
options: You must take a 30-minute break after 8 hours of driving
You must take a 30-minute break at noon, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
You must take a 30-minute break every hour, once the vehicle has been inspected and cleared for operation
You must take a 30-minute break before starting, unless the vehicle is equipped with an automatic safety override
correct_answer: You must take a 30-minute break after 8 hours of driving
explanation: Drivers must take a 30-minute break after 8 cumulative hours of driving without at least a 30-minute interruption.

question: What is a 'logbook'?
options: A fuel log, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
A book about trees, provided all air pressure gauges read within normal operating range
A record of your duty status
A maintenance log, unless the vehicle is equipped with an automatic safety override
correct_answer: A record of your duty status
explanation: A logbook (Record of Duty Status) is a daily record of your activities (driving, on-duty not driving, sleeper berth, off duty).

question: What are the four duty statuses in a logbook?
options: Off duty, sleeper berth, driving, on-duty not driving
Working, resting, eating, sleeping, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Driving, stopped, loading, unloading, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Active, inactive, break,休息, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: Off duty, sleeper berth, driving, on-duty not driving
explanation: The four duty statuses are: Off Duty, Sleeper Berth, Driving, and On-Duty Not Driving.

question: What is an ELD?
options: Electronic Logging Device
Extra Large Driver
Emergency Light Device
Engine Load Detector
correct_answer: Electronic Logging Device
explanation: An ELD (Electronic Logging Device) automatically records driving time and other Hours of Service data.

question: What is the purpose of a 'pre-trip inspection report'?
options: To check fuel levels, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
To document defects found during inspection
To plan your route, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
To log your hours, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: To document defects found during inspection
explanation: A pre-trip inspection report documents any defects or deficiencies found during the vehicle inspection.

question: What must you do if you find a safety defect during your pre-trip inspection?
options: Report it and do not drive until it is fixed
Fix it yourself, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Ignore it if it's minor, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Drive carefully, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: Report it and do not drive until it is fixed
explanation: You must report any safety defects and the vehicle should not be driven until the defect is repaired.

question: What is a 'post-trip inspection'?
options: An inspection done during the trip
An inspection done before the trip
An inspection done at the end of the day
An inspection done by the mechanic
correct_answer: An inspection done at the end of the day
explanation: A post-trip inspection is done at the end of the day to identify any problems that developed during the trip.

question: What should you check in the engine compartment during a pre-trip inspection?
options: Oil level, coolant level, belts, hoses
Radio, GPS, phone charger, provided the load is properly secured and within legal limits
Seats, mirrors, steering wheel
Cargo, straps, tarps, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: Oil level, coolant level, belts, hoses
explanation: Check engine oil level, coolant level, belts (for cracks or fraying), and hoses (for leaks or cracks).

question: What should you look for when inspecting the steering system?
options: The brand of the steering wheel, when operating a vehicle with a GVWR over 26,001 pounds
Missing nuts, cracks, bent or broken parts
The size of the steering wheel, according to the federal motor carrier safety regulations
The color of the steering wheel, provided all air pressure gauges read within normal operating range
correct_answer: Missing nuts, cracks, bent or broken parts
explanation: Look for missing nuts, bolts, cotter keys, or other parts. Look for bent, loose, or broken parts such as the steering column, steering gear box, or tie rods.

question: How much steering wheel play is allowed?
options: No more than 45 degrees, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
As much as you want, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Exactly 5 degrees, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
No more than 10 degrees (about 2 inches on a 20-inch wheel)
correct_answer: No more than 10 degrees (about 2 inches on a 20-inch wheel)
explanation: Steering wheel play should not exceed 10 degrees (approximately 2 inches on a 20-inch steering wheel) before the wheels begin to move.

question: What should you check on the suspension system?
options: The seats, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
The radio, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Springs, spring hangers, shackles, u-bolts, shock absorbers
The paint, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: Springs, spring hangers, shackles, u-bolts, shock absorbers
explanation: Check for cracked or broken spring leaves, broken or missing leaves in a leaf spring, leaves that have shifted so they might hit a tire or other part, missing or broken spring hangers, shackles, u-bolts, or shock absorbers.

question: What is the purpose of the 'fifth wheel'?
options: To carry spare tires, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
To connect the tractor to the trailer
To brake the vehicle, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
To steer the vehicle, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: To connect the tractor to the trailer
explanation: The fifth wheel is the coupling device that connects the tractor to the semi-trailer.

question: What should you check on the fifth wheel?
options: Mounting, platform, locking jaws, release arm
The size, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
The brand, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
The color, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: Mounting, platform, locking jaws, release arm
explanation: Check that the fifth wheel is securely mounted to the frame, the platform is not damaged, locking jaws are around the shank (not the head) of the kingpin, and the release arm is properly seated and the safety latch is engaged.

question: What is the 'kingpin'?
options: The most important person, when hauling hazardous materials over a designated route
The pin that locks into the fifth wheel
A type of bowling pin, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
A steering component, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: The pin that locks into the fifth wheel
explanation: The kingpin is the pin on the trailer that locks into the fifth wheel on the tractor.

question: What should you check on the landing gear?
options: The color, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Fully raised, no missing parts, crank handle secured
The weight, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
The height, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: Fully raised, no missing parts, crank handle secured
explanation: Check that the landing gear is fully raised, no missing parts, and the crank handle is secured.

question: What is 'coupling'?
options: Connecting the tractor to the trailer
Disconnecting the trailer, provided the load is properly secured and within legal limits
Loading cargo, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Parking the truck, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: Connecting the tractor to the trailer
explanation: Coupling is the process of connecting the tractor to the trailer.

question: What is 'uncoupling'?
options: Unloading cargo, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Starting the engine, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Disconnecting the trailer from the tractor
Connecting the trailer, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: Disconnecting the trailer from the tractor
explanation: Uncoupling is the process of disconnecting the trailer from the tractor.

question: Before backing under a trailer, you should:
options: Honk the horn, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Check the trailer height and position
Turn on the radio, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Just back up quickly, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: Check the trailer height and position
explanation: Check that the trailer is at the right height and properly aligned with the tractor before backing under it.

question: After coupling, you should:
options: Check your phone, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Take a break, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Drive away immediately, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Do a tug test to ensure the connection is secure
correct_answer: Do a tug test to ensure the connection is secure
explanation: After coupling, gently pull forward in low gear to test that the trailer is locked onto the tractor.

question: What is a 'glad hand'?
options: A type of glove, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
A friendly greeting, unless the vehicle is equipped with an automatic safety override
A coupling device for air lines
A steering wheel cover, when hauling hazardous materials over a designated route
correct_answer: A coupling device for air lines
explanation: Glad hands are coupling devices used to connect the service and emergency air lines from the truck to the trailer.

question: What color are the glad hands?
options: Both black, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Red for service, blue for emergency
Blue for service, red for emergency
Both silver, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: Blue for service, red for emergency
explanation: The service line glad hand is blue, and the emergency line glad hand is red.

question: Why should you use dummy couplers (glad hand locks) when not towing a trailer?
options: To look professional, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
To keep dirt and water out of the lines
To make noise, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
To save fuel, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: To keep dirt and water out of the lines
explanation: Dummy couplers (glad hand locks) keep dirt and water out of the air lines when not towing a trailer.

question: What is the 'apron' on a fifth wheel?
options: A type of cargo cover, as specified in the state commercial driver licensing manual
The plate the trailer rests on
A safety device, according to the federal motor carrier safety regulations
A cleaning cloth, provided all air pressure gauges read within normal operating range
correct_answer: The plate the trailer rests on
explanation: The apron is the plate on the fifth wheel that the trailer rests on.

question: What should you do before uncoupling a trailer?
options: Remove the wheels
Raise the landing gear
Lower the landing gear
Drain the air tanks
correct_answer: Lower the landing gear
explanation: Before uncoupling, you must lower the landing gear to support the trailer after it is disconnected.

question: What is 'sliding the fifth wheel'?
options: Replacing the fifth wheel, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Cleaning the fifth wheel, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Adjusting the position of the fifth wheel to distribute weight
Removing the fifth wheel, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: Adjusting the position of the fifth wheel to distribute weight
explanation: Sliding the fifth wheel adjusts its position to distribute weight properly between the tractor's steer axle and drive axles.

question: What is 'sliding the tandem axles'?
options: Cleaning the axles, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Replacing the axles, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Adjusting the position of the trailer axles to distribute weight
Removing the axles, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: Adjusting the position of the trailer axles to distribute weight
explanation: Sliding the tandem axles adjusts the position of the trailer's rear axles to distribute weight and comply with bridge laws.

question: What is a 'bridge formula'?
options: A formula that limits the weight on groups of axles
A mathematical equation, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
A formula for building bridges, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
A recipe, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: A formula that limits the weight on groups of axles
explanation: The bridge formula is a federal law that limits the weight-to-length ratio of a vehicle to protect bridges and roads.

question: What is the maximum gross vehicle weight allowed on most interstate highways?
options: 100,000 pounds
60,000 pounds
80,000 pounds
50,000 pounds
correct_answer: 80,000 pounds
explanation: The federal limit for gross vehicle weight on interstate highways is 80,000 pounds (without special permits).

question: What is the maximum weight allowed on a single axle?
options: 20,000 pounds
40,000 pounds
34,000 pounds
12,000 pounds
correct_answer: 20,000 pounds
explanation: The federal limit for a single axle is 20,000 pounds.

question: What is the maximum weight allowed on tandem axles?
options: 40,000 pounds
50,000 pounds
34,000 pounds
20,000 pounds
correct_answer: 34,000 pounds
explanation: The federal limit for tandem axles is 34,000 pounds.

question: Where can you find the GVWR of a vehicle?
options: On the vehicle certification label
On the license plate, as specified in the state commercial driver licensing manual
On the windshield, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
In the owner's manual only
correct_answer: On the vehicle certification label
explanation: The GVWR is listed on the vehicle certification label, usually found on the driver's door frame.

question: What is 'axle weight'?
options: The weight transmitted to the ground by one axle or axle set
The weight of the axle itself, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
The weight of the wheels, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
The maximum weight the axle can hold, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: The weight transmitted to the ground by one axle or axle set
explanation: Axle weight is the total weight transmitted to the ground by all wheels on an axle or axle set.

question: What happens if you are overweight on an axle?
options: You can be fined and required to offload cargo
You get a warning, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
You can keep driving, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Nothing, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: You can be fined and required to offload cargo
explanation: Being overweight can result in fines and you may be required to offload cargo before continuing.

question: What is a 'weigh station'?
options: A place where commercial vehicles are weighed
A place to buy weight loss products, as specified in the state commercial driver licensing manual
A restaurant, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
A gym, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: A place where commercial vehicles are weighed
explanation: A weigh station is a checkpoint where commercial vehicles are weighed to ensure they comply with weight regulations.

question: Must you stop at all weigh stations?
options: Only if you think you are overweight, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
No, they are optional, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Yes, if the signs indicate your vehicle type must stop
Only at night, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: Yes, if the signs indicate your vehicle type must stop
explanation: You must stop at weigh stations when signs indicate that vehicles of your type must stop.

question: What is a 'portable scale'?
options: A scale you carry in your truck, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
A bathroom scale, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
A digital scale, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
A scale that can be moved to different locations
correct_answer: A scale that can be moved to different locations
explanation: Portable scales can be set up at various locations for temporary weight enforcement.

question: What is 'cargo securement'?
options: Counting the cargo, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Insuring the cargo, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Locking the trailer doors, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
The process of securing cargo to prevent movement
correct_answer: The process of securing cargo to prevent movement
explanation: Cargo securement involves using tie-downs, chains, straps, and other devices to prevent cargo from shifting or falling off the vehicle.

question: What is the minimum working load limit for tie-downs?
options: 1/2 the weight of the cargo being secured
1/4 the weight of the cargo, as long as all safety systems have been tested and verified
Equal to the weight of the cargo, provided all air pressure gauges read within normal operating range
Twice the weight of the cargo, according to the federal motor carrier safety regulations
correct_answer: 1/2 the weight of the cargo being secured
explanation: The aggregate working load limit of all tie-downs must be at least 1/2 the weight of the cargo.

question: How often should you check your cargo securement during a trip?
options: Never, if properly secured initially, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Only at the end, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Once at the beginning, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Within the first 50 miles and every 3 hours or 150 miles thereafter
correct_answer: Within the first 50 miles and every 3 hours or 150 miles thereafter
explanation: You must inspect cargo and securement devices within the first 50 miles of a trip and every 3 hours or 150 miles thereafter.

question: What is a 'tiedown'?
options: A trailer hitch, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
A parking brake, when operating a vehicle with a GVWR over 26,001 pounds
A type of knot, when operating a vehicle with a GVWR over 26,001 pounds
A device used to secure cargo
correct_answer: A device used to secure cargo
explanation: A tie-down is a chain, strap, or other device used to secure cargo to a vehicle.

question: What is 'edge protection'?
options: A type of insurance, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
A warning sign, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
A safety rail, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Material placed between cargo and tie-downs to prevent damage
correct_answer: Material placed between cargo and tie-downs to prevent damage
explanation: Edge protection prevents tie-downs from being cut or damaged by sharp edges on cargo.

question: What is 'header board' (headache rack)?
options: A barrier between the cab and cargo to protect the driver
A type of cargo, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
A sign on the front of the truck, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
A dashboard, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: A barrier between the cab and cargo to protect the driver
explanation: A header board (headache rack) protects the driver from cargo shifting forward in a sudden stop.

question: Can you use damaged tie-downs?
options: Only for light loads, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Yes, if they still work, following established DOT pre-trip inspection requirements
Yes, if you use more of them, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
No, damaged tie-downs must be replaced
correct_answer: No, damaged tie-downs must be replaced
explanation: Never use damaged tie-downs. They may fail and cause cargo to shift or fall.

question: What is 'indirect tiedown'?
options: A temporary tie-down, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
A tie-down attached directly to the cargo
A tie-down that goes over or around the cargo
A broken tie-down, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: A tie-down that goes over or around the cargo
explanation: An indirect tie-down goes over or around the cargo and attaches to the vehicle, creating downward force.

question: What is 'direct tiedown'?
options: A tie-down that goes over the cargo
A chain, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
A tie-down attached directly to the cargo
A strap, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: A tie-down attached directly to the cargo
explanation: A direct tie-down is attached directly to the cargo and to the vehicle, preventing movement.

question: What is the purpose of a 'cargo cover' (tarp)?
options: To make the truck look nice
To secure the cargo, unless the vehicle is equipped with an automatic safety override
To hide the cargo, provided all air pressure gauges read within normal operating range
To protect cargo from weather
correct_answer: To protect cargo from weather
explanation: A cargo cover (tarp) protects cargo from rain, snow, and other weather conditions.

question: What should you do if cargo contains liquids?
options: Open the containers, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Drive faster, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Ignore it, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Ensure containers are sealed and secured to prevent leaks
correct_answer: Ensure containers are sealed and secured to prevent leaks
explanation: Liquid cargo must be in sealed containers and properly secured to prevent leaks and spills.

question: What is 'load distribution'?
options: Spreading the weight evenly across axles
Loading cargo quickly, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Counting cargo items, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Unloading cargo, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: Spreading the weight evenly across axles
explanation: Load distribution means spreading the weight of cargo evenly to avoid overloading any axle and to maintain vehicle stability.

question: Where should you place the heaviest cargo?
options: High and to one side
At the back
Low and centered
At the front
correct_answer: Low and centered
explanation: Place the heaviest cargo low and centered to keep the center of gravity low and maintain stability.

question: What is a 'rollover'?
options: A type of accident report, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
A driving maneuver, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
When a vehicle tips over onto its side or roof
A type of cargo, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: When a vehicle tips over onto its side or roof
explanation: A rollover occurs when a vehicle tips over, often due to high center of gravity, excessive speed in turns, or improper load distribution.

question: What increases the risk of a rollover?
options: Flat roads, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Driving slowly, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
High center of gravity, excessive speed, sharp turns
Empty truck, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: High center of gravity, excessive speed, sharp turns
explanation: High center of gravity, excessive speed in curves, and sharp turns all increase the risk of a rollover.

question: What is a 'ramp'?
options: A parking area, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
A toll booth, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
A type of cargo, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
An inclined surface for loading or emergency stops
correct_answer: An inclined surface for loading or emergency stops
explanation: A ramp can be a loading ramp or an escape ramp designed to stop runaway vehicles.

question: What is an 'escape ramp'?
options: A loading dock, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
A ramp designed to stop runaway vehicles
A parking ramp, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
An exit from a highway, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: A ramp designed to stop runaway vehicles
explanation: An escape ramp is a specially designed area on steep downgrades to safely stop vehicles that have lost their brakes.

question: What should you do if you see an escape ramp sign?
options: Note its location in case you need it
Ignore it, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Use it immediately, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Take a photo, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: Note its location in case you need it
explanation: Note the location of escape ramps so you know where they are if your brakes fail on a downgrade.

question: What is 'black ice'?
options: Dirty ice, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Ice in the shade, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
A thin, nearly invisible layer of ice on the road
Ice at night, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: A thin, nearly invisible layer of ice on the road
explanation: Black ice is a thin, transparent layer of ice on the road surface that is very difficult to see and extremely slippery.

question: When is black ice most likely to form?
options: When the temperature is around freezing and the road is wet
When it's very cold, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Only at night, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
In summer, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: When the temperature is around freezing and the road is wet
explanation: Black ice forms when the temperature is near or just below freezing and moisture on the road freezes into a thin layer.

question: Which surfaces freeze first?
options: Highways, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
City streets, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Bridges, overpasses, and shaded areas
Parking lots, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: Bridges, overpasses, and shaded areas
explanation: Bridges and overpasses freeze before other road surfaces because cold air circulates both above and below them.

question: What should you do when driving in winter conditions?
options: Use cruise control, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Tailgate for better traction, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Reduce speed, increase following distance, be gentle with controls
Drive faster to get through quickly, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: Reduce speed, increase following distance, be gentle with controls
explanation: In winter conditions, slow down, increase following distance, and use gentle movements with the steering wheel, brakes, and accelerator.

question: What are 'tire chains'?
options: Chains to measure tire pressure, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Chains placed on tires for better traction in snow and ice
Decorative chains, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Chains to lock tires, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: Chains placed on tires for better traction in snow and ice
explanation: Tire chains are placed on drive wheels to provide better traction in snow and ice.

question: When should you use tire chains?
options: Only on flat roads, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
When required by law or when conditions are very slippery
