correct_answer: Constantly scan, don't fixate on one thing
explanation: Keep Your Eyes Moving means constantly scanning your environment rather than fixating on any single object.

question: What does 'Leave Yourself an Out' mean?
options: Park, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Quit driving, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Always have an escape route planned
Exit the vehicle, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: Always have an escape route planned
explanation: Leave Yourself an Out means always having an escape route or plan B if your intended path becomes blocked.

question: What does 'Make Sure They See You' mean?
options: Flash lights, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Honk constantly, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Communicate your presence and intentions to others
Wave at everyone, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: Communicate your presence and intentions to others
explanation: Make Sure They See You means using signals, lights, horn, and positioning to ensure others know you're there and what you're doing.

question: What is the foundation of all professional commercial driving?
options: Just a license, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Just following rules, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Safety, knowledge, skill, and professional conduct working together
Just experience, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: Safety, knowledge, skill, and professional conduct working together
explanation: Professional commercial driving is built on the foundation of safety commitment, comprehensive knowledge, exceptional skill, and professional conduct.

question: What is the ultimate goal of every professional commercial driver?
options: Delivering on time at any cost
Driving fast, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Getting everyone home safely every day
Making money, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: Getting everyone home safely every day
explanation: The ultimate goal of every professional driver is ensuring everyone - yourself, other road users, and your passengers - gets home safely every single day.

question: What is the biggest hazard of night driving?
options: More traffic
Reduced visibility
Drunk drivers
Road construction
correct_answer: Reduced visibility
explanation: The biggest hazard of night driving is reduced visibility. You cannot see as far or as clearly as during the day.

question: How far can you see with low beams?
options: About 500 feet
About 100 feet
About 250 feet
About 1000 feet
correct_answer: About 250 feet
explanation: Low beams typically let you see about 250 feet ahead. You should not drive faster than you can stop within that distance.

question: How far can you see with high beams?
options: About 200 feet
About 1 mile
About 1000 feet
About 350-500 feet
correct_answer: About 350-500 feet
explanation: High beams typically let you see about 350-500 feet ahead. Use them whenever safe and legal to do so.

question: When must you dim your high beams for oncoming traffic?
options: Never, unless the vehicle is equipped with an automatic safety override
Within 200 feet
Within 100 feet
Within 500 feet
correct_answer: Within 500 feet
explanation: You must dim your high beams within 500 feet of an oncoming vehicle to avoid blinding the other driver.

question: When must you dim your high beams when following another vehicle?
options: Within 100 feet, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Within 500 feet (some states vary)
Never, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Within 200 feet, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: Within 500 feet (some states vary)
explanation: Generally, dim high beams within 500 feet when following another vehicle, though state laws may vary (e.g., 200-300 feet).

question: What should you do if an oncoming driver doesn't dim their high beams?
options: Flash your high beams back, provided the load is properly secured and within legal limits
Look to the right edge of your lane
Turn on your high beams, when hauling hazardous materials over a designated route
Look straight at them, following established DOT pre-trip inspection requirements
correct_answer: Look to the right edge of your lane
explanation: If an oncoming driver fails to dim, look to the right edge of your lane or the white line to avoid being blinded.

question: What is 'overdriving your headlights'?
options: Using high beams too much, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Driving too slow, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Driving with broken lights, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Driving so fast you can't stop within the distance you can see
correct_answer: Driving so fast you can't stop within the distance you can see
explanation: Overdriving your headlights means driving at a speed where your stopping distance is longer than the distance your lights illuminate.

question: What is the best advice for driving in fog?
options: Use high beams, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Drive fast to get out of it, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Don't drive in fog if possible; pull off safely
Follow close to other cars, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: Don't drive in fog if possible; pull off safely
explanation: The best advice for fog is to avoid driving in it. If you must, use low beams, slow down, and be prepared to stop.

question: Which lights should you use in fog?
options: High beams, when hauling hazardous materials over a designated route
Low beams or fog lights
Parking lights only
Hazard lights only
correct_answer: Low beams or fog lights
explanation: Use low beams or fog lights in fog. High beams reflect off the water droplets and cause glare, reducing visibility further.

question: What is 'black ice'?
options: Oil on the road, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Asphalt, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Thin, clear ice that looks like wet road
Dirty ice, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: Thin, clear ice that looks like wet road
explanation: Black ice is a thin layer of clear ice that makes the road look wet. It is extremely slippery and dangerous.

question: How can you detect black ice?
options: Touch the road, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Check thermometer, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Ask other drivers, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Check mirror mounts or antenna for ice buildup; road looks wet but no spray
correct_answer: Check mirror mounts or antenna for ice buildup; road looks wet but no spray
explanation: Clues for black ice include ice building up on mirror mounts/antennas, and the road looking wet but tires not creating spray.

question: When are roads most slippery during rain?
options: Just after it starts raining
When it stops raining
During heavy downpour
After it has rained for an hour
correct_answer: Just after it starts raining
explanation: Roads are most slippery just after rain begins because the water mixes with oil and grease on the road surface.

question: What is 'hydroplaning'?
options: Washing the truck, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Driving through deep water, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Tires lose contact with road and ride on a layer of water
Water cooling, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: Tires lose contact with road and ride on a layer of water
explanation: Hydroplaning occurs when water builds up under tires, lifting them off the road surface and causing total loss of traction.

question: What should you do if you hydroplane?
options: Accelerate, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Brake hard, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Release accelerator, don't brake, steer slightly if needed
Steer sharply, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: Release accelerator, don't brake, steer slightly if needed
explanation: If hydroplaning, release the accelerator to slow down. Do not brake or steer sharply as this can cause a skid.

question: How much should you reduce speed on wet roads?
options: No reduction needed
By about 1/3
By half
Stop completely
correct_answer: By about 1/3
explanation: On wet roads, reduce your speed by about one-third (e.g., from 55 to 35 mph) to maintain traction and stopping distance.

question: How much should you reduce speed on packed snow?
options: By 1/3, as specified in the state commercial driver licensing manual
By at least 1/2
No reduction needed
Drive 5 mph
correct_answer: By at least 1/2
explanation: On packed snow, reduce your speed by at least half (e.g., from 55 to 25-30 mph) to compensate for reduced traction.

question: How much should you reduce speed on ice?
options: By 1/3, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
By 1/2, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Drive normal speed, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Slow to a crawl and stop as soon as safely possible
correct_answer: Slow to a crawl and stop as soon as safely possible
explanation: On ice, reduce speed to a crawl and stop driving as soon as you can safely do so. It is extremely dangerous.

question: What should you check before driving in winter?
options: Coolant, defrosters, wipers, tires, lights, and chains
Seat covers, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
AC only, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Radio, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: Coolant, defrosters, wipers, tires, lights, and chains
explanation: Winter checks include coolant level/antifreeze, defrosters, wipers/washers, tires, lights, and ensuring you have tire chains.

question: Why shouldn't you use the engine brake on slippery surfaces?
options: It wastes fuel, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
It can cause the drive wheels to skid
It damages the engine, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
It's illegal, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: It can cause the drive wheels to skid
explanation: Avoid using the engine brake (retarder) on slippery roads because it can lock the drive wheels and cause a skid or jackknife.

question: What is the danger of driving through deep puddles?
options: Getting the truck dirty, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Water can affect brakes, causing them to grab or fail
Splashing pedestrians, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Rust, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: Water can affect brakes, causing them to grab or fail
explanation: Water can enter brake drums, causing brakes to be weak, apply unevenly, or grab. Avoid deep water if possible.

question: How do you dry wet brakes?
options: Slow down, apply light pressure to brakes while driving
Accelerate quickly to build up system pressure before stopping
Stop and wait, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Use air dryer, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: Slow down, apply light pressure to brakes while driving
explanation: To dry wet brakes, slow down and apply light pressure to the brake pedal for a short distance to heat up and dry the linings.

question: What happens to tires in hot weather?
options: Air pressure decreases
Air pressure increases
They shrink, as long as all safety systems have been tested and verified
Nothing, when hauling hazardous materials over a designated route
correct_answer: Air pressure increases
explanation: Heat causes air in tires to expand, increasing pressure. Do not let air out, as pressure will be too low when they cool.

question: How often should you check tires in hot weather?
options: Every 2 hours or 100 miles
Once a day, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Every 4 hours, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Never, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: Every 2 hours or 100 miles
explanation: In very hot weather, check tires every 2 hours or 100 miles to ensure they aren't overheating or failing.

question: What is 'bleeding tar'?
options: Tar rising to road surface in extreme heat, making it slippery
Road repair, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Leaking cargo, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Oil spill, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: Tar rising to road surface in extreme heat, making it slippery
explanation: In extreme heat, tar in the pavement can rise to the surface ('bleed'), making the road very slippery.

question: What is 'brake fade'?
options: Loss of braking power due to overheating
Old brake fluid, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Worn brake pads, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Broken brake line, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: Loss of braking power due to overheating
explanation: Brake fade is the reduction in stopping power that occurs when brakes get too hot, usually from overuse on downgrades.

question: How do you prevent brake fade on downgrades?
options: Use brakes constantly, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Shift to neutral, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Use a low gear and engine braking; use brakes sparingly
Accelerate quickly to build up system pressure before stopping
correct_answer: Use a low gear and engine braking; use brakes sparingly
explanation: Prevent brake fade by selecting a low gear before the descent and using engine braking as the primary slowing force.

question: What is the proper braking technique on long downgrades?
options: Pump the brakes, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Ride the brakes, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Snub braking: Brake to 5 mph below safe speed, release, repeat
Don't brake, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: Snub braking: Brake to 5 mph below safe speed, release, repeat
explanation: Use snub braking: Apply brakes firmly to slow 5 mph below safe speed, release to let them cool, and repeat when speed increases.

question: What is an 'escape ramp'?
options: On ramp, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Exit ramp, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Rest area, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Emergency lane for vehicles with failed brakes to stop safely
correct_answer: Emergency lane for vehicles with failed brakes to stop safely
explanation: Escape ramps (runaway truck ramps) are designed to safely stop vehicles that have lost their brakes on steep downgrades.

question: When should you use an escape ramp?
options: Never, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
For parking, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
As soon as you realize your brakes have failed
Only as a last resort, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: As soon as you realize your brakes have failed
explanation: Use an escape ramp immediately if you lose your brakes. Do not wait until speed becomes uncontrollable.

question: What causes most tire fires?
options: New tires, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Cold weather, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Under-inflation and duals touching
Over-inflation, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: Under-inflation and duals touching
explanation: Under-inflated tires generate excessive heat, which can cause fires. Dual tires touching due to under-inflation is a major cause.

question: What is a 'Class A' fire?
options: Wood, paper, ordinary combustibles
Liquids, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Electrical, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Metals, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: Wood, paper, ordinary combustibles
explanation: Class A fires involve ordinary combustibles like wood, paper, cloth, and rubber.

question: What is a 'Class B' fire?
options: Flammable liquids and gases
Wood, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Electrical, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Metals, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: Flammable liquids and gases
explanation: Class B fires involve flammable liquids like gasoline, oil, grease, and diesel fuel.

question: What is a 'Class C' fire?
options: Electrical equipment
Metals, after verifying all brake components are properly adjusted
Liquids, following established DOT pre-trip inspection requirements
Wood, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: Electrical equipment
explanation: Class C fires involve energized electrical equipment.

question: What type of fire extinguisher must a truck carry?
options: B:C or A:B:C
None required
Sand
Water
correct_answer: B:C or A:B:C
explanation: CMVs must carry a fire extinguisher suitable for Class B and C fires (B:C) or A, B, and C fires (A:B:C).

question: What should you do if your engine is on fire?
options: Pour water on it, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Drive faster, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Open hood immediately, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Turn off engine, don't open hood, shoot foam through louvers/radiator
correct_answer: Turn off engine, don't open hood, shoot foam through louvers/radiator
explanation: Turn off the engine. Do NOT open the hood (adds oxygen). Shoot extinguisher foam through the radiator or louvers.

question: What should you do for a cargo fire in a van/box trailer?
options: Drive to fire station, as specified in the state commercial driver licensing manual
Open doors to see, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Ignore it, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Keep doors closed to limit oxygen
correct_answer: Keep doors closed to limit oxygen
explanation: Keep trailer doors closed to limit oxygen supply. Opening doors can cause a flashover or intensify the fire.

question: What is the 'PASS' method for using an extinguisher?
options: Point, Aim, Shoot, Spray
Push, Aim, Spray, Stop
Pull, Aim, Squeeze, Sweep
Pull, Ask, See, Stop
correct_answer: Pull, Aim, Squeeze, Sweep
explanation: PASS stands for: Pull the pin, Aim at the base of fire, Squeeze the handle, Sweep side to side.

question: What is 'skid'?
options: A type of turn, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
A parking method, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
A brake type, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
When tires lose grip on the road
correct_answer: When tires lose grip on the road
explanation: A skid happens when tires lose their grip on the road, usually due to over-braking, over-steering, over-acceleration, or driving too fast.

question: What is the most common cause of skids?
options: Driving too fast for conditions
Wind, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Bad brakes, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Bad tires, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: Driving too fast for conditions
explanation: The most common cause of skids is driving too fast for road conditions.

question: How do you correct a rear-wheel skid?
options: Stop braking/accelerating, steer in direction of skid (counter-steer)
Steer opposite to skid, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Accelerate, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Brake hard, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: Stop braking/accelerating, steer in direction of skid (counter-steer)
explanation: Stop braking or accelerating. Steer in the direction the rear of the vehicle is sliding (counter-steer) to regain control.

question: What is a 'front-wheel skid'?
options: Trailer slide, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Rear wheels slide, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Spin out, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Front wheels lose traction, vehicle goes straight regardless of steering
correct_answer: Front wheels lose traction, vehicle goes straight regardless of steering
explanation: In a front-wheel skid (understeer), the front wheels lose traction and the vehicle continues straight regardless of steering input.

question: How do you correct a front-wheel skid?
options: Steer harder, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Slow down, stop turning until traction returns
Brake hard, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Accelerate, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: Slow down, stop turning until traction returns
explanation: To correct a front-wheel skid, let off the accelerator to slow down. You may need to straighten the wheels slightly to regain traction.

question: What should you do if a tire blows out?
options: Steer sharply, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Hold steering wheel firmly, stay off brake, slow down naturally
Brake hard immediately, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Accelerate, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: Hold steering wheel firmly, stay off brake, slow down naturally
explanation: If a tire blows, hold the steering wheel firmly to maintain control. Do NOT brake. Let the vehicle slow down naturally, then pull over.

question: What are signs of a tire blowout?
options: Loud bang, vibration, heavy steering (front), fishtailing (rear)
Engine noise, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Smoke, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Brake squeal, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: Loud bang, vibration, heavy steering (front), fishtailing (rear)
explanation: Signs include a loud bang, heavy vibration. Front blowout causes heavy steering; rear blowout causes fishtailing.

question: What is 'BAC'?
options: Blood Alcohol Concentration
Big Air Canister, according to the federal motor carrier safety regulations
Brake Air Compressor
Basic Air Control, unless the vehicle is equipped with an automatic safety override
correct_answer: Blood Alcohol Concentration
explanation: BAC stands for Blood Alcohol Concentration, a measure of the amount of alcohol in your body.

question: What is the legal BAC limit for CDL drivers?
explanation: The legal limit for CDL drivers operating a CMV is 0.04%, half the limit for regular drivers.

question: What happens if you refuse a drug/alcohol test?
options: You can take it later, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
You get a warning, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
It is treated as a positive result/failure
Nothing, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: It is treated as a positive result/failure
explanation: Refusing a required drug or alcohol test is treated the same as a positive result or failure, leading to disqualification.

question: Can you drink alcohol while on duty?
options: Only beer
Only if parked
No, never
Yes, with lunch
correct_answer: No, never
explanation: You cannot drink alcohol while on duty or within 4 hours of reporting for duty.

question: What determines how fast alcohol leaves your body?
options: Coffee
Cold shower
Exercise
Only time
correct_answer: Only time
explanation: Only time can remove alcohol from your body. Coffee, showers, or exercise do not speed up the process.

question: What is 'implied consent'?
options: You agree to pay fines, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
You agree to be searched, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
By driving, you agree to take chemical tests if requested
You agree to work, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: By driving, you agree to take chemical tests if requested
explanation: Implied consent means that by holding a CDL and driving, you automatically consent to drug/alcohol testing if suspected.

question: What should you do if you are drowsy?
options: Turn up radio
Pull over and sleep
Open window
Drink coffee
correct_answer: Pull over and sleep
explanation: The only cure for drowsiness is sleep. Pull over in a safe place and take a nap or sleep.

question: What is 'highway hypnosis'?
options: Hallucinating, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Trance-like state from driving long distances on open roads
Daydreaming, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Sleeping, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: Trance-like state from driving long distances on open roads
explanation: Highway hypnosis is a trance-like state caused by monotony. Avoid it by moving eyes, taking breaks, and keeping cool.

question: How often should you take breaks?
options: Every 4 hours, as long as all safety systems have been tested and verified
Every 8 hours, once the vehicle has been inspected and cleared for operation
Every 2 hours or 100 miles
Only when tired, only after completing a thorough pre-trip safety inspection
correct_answer: Every 2 hours or 100 miles
explanation: Take a break every 2 hours or 100 miles to stretch, walk around, and stay alert.

question: What is 'distracted driving'?
options: Listening to radio, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Talking to passenger, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Any activity that takes attention away from driving
Looking at mirrors, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: Any activity that takes attention away from driving
explanation: Distracted driving is any activity that diverts attention from driving, including texting, eating, or using devices.

question: Can you text while driving a CMV?
options: Yes, if careful, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
No, it is strictly prohibited
Only with hands-free, after verifying all brake components are properly adjusted
Only at red lights, as specified in the state commercial driver licensing manual
correct_answer: No, it is strictly prohibited
explanation: Texting while driving a CMV is strictly prohibited by federal law and carries heavy penalties.

question: Can you use a hand-held phone while driving a CMV?
options: Yes, briefly, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
No, you must use hands-free devices
Yes, for business, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Yes, in emergencies, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: No, you must use hands-free devices
explanation: Using a hand-held mobile phone is prohibited. You must use a hands-free device and one-touch operation.

question: What is 'aggressive driving'?
options: Careful driving, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Slow driving, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Operating a vehicle in a bold or pushy manner
Defensive driving, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: Operating a vehicle in a bold or pushy manner
explanation: Aggressive driving includes speeding, tailgating, unsafe lane changes, and ignoring traffic controls.

question: What should you do if confronted by an aggressive driver?
options: Yell back, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Avoid eye contact, give them space, don't engage
Block them, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Honk, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: Avoid eye contact, give them space, don't engage
explanation: Do not engage with aggressive drivers. Avoid eye contact, give them plenty of room, and call police if safe.

question: What is a 'medical certificate'?
options: Insurance card, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Document proving you are physically qualified to drive
Doctor's note, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Prescription, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: Document proving you are physically qualified to drive
explanation: A medical examiner's certificate (medical card) proves you meet the physical requirements to operate a CMV.

question: How long is a medical certificate valid?
options: 5 years
Forever
1 year, only when driving under a special state or federal permit
Up to 24 months
correct_answer: Up to 24 months
explanation: A medical certificate is valid for up to 24 months, but may be issued for less time if you have certain medical conditions.

question: What is 'sleep apnea'?
options: Insomnia, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Snoring, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Nightmares, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Condition where breathing stops repeatedly during sleep
correct_answer: Condition where breathing stops repeatedly during sleep
explanation: Sleep apnea is a serious condition where breathing stops repeatedly during sleep, causing fatigue. It must be treated to drive.

question: What are 'Hours of Service' (HOS)?
options: Shop hours, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Pay hours, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Regulations limiting driving and on-duty time
Delivery times, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: Regulations limiting driving and on-duty time
explanation: Hours of Service regulations limit the number of hours you can drive and work to prevent fatigue.

question: What is the '11-hour rule'?
options: You may drive max 11 hours after 10 consecutive hours off
You can work 11 hours, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
You can drive 11 hours straight, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
You must sleep 11 hours, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: You may drive max 11 hours after 10 consecutive hours off
explanation: The 11-hour rule states you may drive a maximum of 11 hours after 10 consecutive hours off duty.

question: What is the '14-hour rule'?
options: You must work 14 hours, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Day is 14 hours long, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
You must sleep 14 hours, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
You may not drive after the 14th hour of coming on duty
correct_answer: You may not drive after the 14th hour of coming on duty
explanation: The 14-hour rule states you may not drive beyond the 14th consecutive hour after coming on duty.

question: What is the '60/70-hour rule'?
options: Speed limit, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Age limit, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Weight limit, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Limit on on-duty hours in 7 or 8 consecutive days
correct_answer: Limit on on-duty hours in 7 or 8 consecutive days
explanation: You may not drive after 60/70 hours on duty in 7/8 consecutive days.

question: What is the '30-minute break' rule?
options: Break every 30 mins, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Nap time, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Lunch break, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Must take 30-min break after 8 cumulative driving hours
correct_answer: Must take 30-min break after 8 cumulative driving hours
explanation: You must take a 30-minute break if more than 8 consecutive hours have passed since your last off-duty period of at least 30 minutes.

question: What is a 'logbook' or 'ELD'?
options: Maintenance log, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Diary, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Fuel log, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Record of your duty status
correct_answer: Record of your duty status
explanation: A logbook or Electronic Logging Device (ELD) records your duty status (Driving, On-Duty, Off-Duty, Sleeper Berth) for HOS compliance.

question: What is 'On-Duty' time?
options: Lunch time, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Only loading time, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Only driving time, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
All time you are working or ready to work
correct_answer: All time you are working or ready to work
explanation: On-Duty time includes all time from when you begin work until you are relieved, including driving, loading, inspecting, and waiting.

question: What is 'Off-Duty' time?
options: Waiting for load, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Lunch break, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Fueling, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Time when you are relieved of all responsibility
correct_answer: Time when you are relieved of all responsibility
explanation: Off-Duty time is when you are relieved of all responsibility and are free to do what you want.

question: What is 'Sleeper Berth' time?
options: Time spent resting in the sleeper berth
Off duty, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Sleeping in hotel, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Sleeping in seat, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: Time spent resting in the sleeper berth
explanation: Sleeper Berth time is time spent resting in the vehicle's sleeper berth, which counts towards off-duty requirements.

question: What is a 'hazardous material'?
options: Liquid cargo, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Heavy cargo, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Any cargo, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Substance posing risk to health, safety, and property
correct_answer: Substance posing risk to health, safety, and property
explanation: Hazardous materials are substances that pose a risk to health, safety, and property during transportation.

question: What are 'placards'?
options: License plates, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Company logos, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Diamond-shaped signs identifying hazardous materials
Warning lights, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: Diamond-shaped signs identifying hazardous materials
explanation: Placards are diamond-shaped signs placed on the outside of vehicles to identify the hazardous materials they are carrying.

question: Who is responsible for placarding?
options: Mechanic, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Receiver, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Shipper offers them, driver affixes them
Police, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: Shipper offers them, driver affixes them
explanation: The shipper provides the placards, but the driver is responsible for ensuring they are properly affixed to the vehicle.

question: Can you drive a placarded vehicle without a Hazmat endorsement?
options: Yes, with supervisor
Yes, if empty
Yes, if short distance
No, never
correct_answer: No, never
explanation: You must have a Hazardous Materials (H) endorsement on your CDL to drive any vehicle requiring placards.

question: What should you do if you have a hazmat spill?
options: Ignore it, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Drive the vehicle away immediately after uncoupling the trailer
Park safely, secure area, warn others, notify authorities
Clean it up yourself, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: Park safely, secure area, warn others, notify authorities
explanation: If a spill occurs, park safely, secure the area, warn others, and notify authorities. Do not try to clean it unless trained.

question: Where should shipping papers be kept?
options: In trunk, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
With cargo, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
In door pouch or on seat within reach while driving
In glove box, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: In door pouch or on seat within reach while driving
explanation: Hazmat shipping papers must be within reach while driving (door pouch or seat) and visible on the seat when you leave the vehicle.

question: What is 'containment rules'?
options: Rules for packaging, labeling, and placarding hazmat
Driving rules, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Parking rules, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Loading rules, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: Rules for packaging, labeling, and placarding hazmat
explanation: Containment rules tell shippers how to package and label hazmat and drivers how to load and placard it.

question: What is 'segregation' of hazmat?
options: Sorting by weight, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Keeping incompatible materials apart
Sorting by color, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Sorting by destination, when hauling hazardous materials over a designated route
correct_answer: Keeping incompatible materials apart
explanation: Segregation means keeping incompatible hazardous materials apart to prevent dangerous reactions.

question: What is the 'Emergency Response Guidebook' (ERG)?
options: Phone book, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Rule book, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Guide for first responders on hazmat incidents
Map book, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: Guide for first responders on hazmat incidents
explanation: The ERG is a guidebook for first responders to identify hazards and protect themselves during the initial phase of a hazmat incident.

question: What is 'accident reporting'?
options: Notifying authorities and company of crashes
Taking photos, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Writing a story, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Calling home, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: Notifying authorities and company of crashes
explanation: You must report accidents involving death, injury, or towing to authorities and your company immediately.

question: What information should you exchange at an accident?
options: Name, address, license, insurance, vehicle info
Phone number only, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Money, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Apologies, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: Name, address, license, insurance, vehicle info
explanation: Exchange name, address, driver's license number, insurance information, and vehicle details with other parties involved.

question: Should you admit fault at an accident scene?
options: Always, as specified in the state commercial driver licensing manual
Yes, be polite
Yes, if you think so
No, stick to facts
correct_answer: No, stick to facts
explanation: Do not admit fault at the scene. Stick to the facts of what happened and let authorities determine liability.

question: What should you do first at an accident scene?
options: Call police, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Check for injuries, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Call boss, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Protect the area (warning devices)
correct_answer: Protect the area (warning devices)
explanation: First, protect the area to prevent further accidents. Then notify authorities and care for the injured.

question: How do you protect the accident area?
options: Yell, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Wave arms, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Stand in road, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Turn on flashers, set out triangles/flares
correct_answer: Turn on flashers, set out triangles/flares
explanation: Use hazard lights and set out emergency warning devices (triangles, flares) to warn other drivers.

question: Where do you place triangles on a two-lane road?
options: All in back, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
100 ft front, 10 ft rear, 100 ft rear
Around truck, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
All in front, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: 100 ft front, 10 ft rear, 100 ft rear
explanation: On a two-lane road: one triangle 100 ft ahead, one 10 ft behind, and one 100 ft behind the vehicle.

question: Where do you place triangles on a divided highway?
options: 10 ft, 100 ft, and 200 ft behind vehicle
500 ft back, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Beside vehicle, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Front and back, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: 10 ft, 100 ft, and 200 ft behind vehicle
explanation: On a divided highway: place triangles at 10 ft, 100 ft, and 200 ft behind the vehicle.

question: Where do you place triangles if view is obstructed?
options: Put on roof, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Don't use them, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Move the rear triangle back (100-500 ft) to be seen
Keep at 100 ft, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: Move the rear triangle back (100-500 ft) to be seen
explanation: If a hill or curve obstructs the view, move the rear triangle back (up to 500 ft) so approaching drivers can see it.

question: What is 'Gross Vehicle Weight' (GVW)?
options: Total weight of a single vehicle plus its load
Weight of load only, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Weight of empty vehicle, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Weight rating, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: Total weight of a single vehicle plus its load
explanation: GVW is the total weight of a single vehicle plus its load.

question: What is 'Gross Combination Weight' (GCW)?
options: Weight of truck only, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Total weight of a powered unit, trailer(s), and load
Weight of trailer only, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Weight rating, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: Total weight of a powered unit, trailer(s), and load
explanation: GCW is the total weight of a powered unit (tractor), plus trailer(s), plus the cargo.

question: What is 'Gross Vehicle Weight Rating' (GVWR)?
options: Actual weight, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Minimum weight, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Axle weight, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Maximum GVW specified by manufacturer
correct_answer: Maximum GVW specified by manufacturer
explanation: GVWR is the maximum GVW specified by the manufacturer for a single vehicle.

question: What is 'Gross Combination Weight Rating' (GCWR)?
options: Minimum weight, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Trailer weight, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Actual weight, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Maximum GCW specified by manufacturer
correct_answer: Maximum GCW specified by manufacturer
explanation: GCWR is the maximum GCW specified by the manufacturer for a specific combination of vehicles.

question: What is 'tare weight'?
options: Axle weight, provided the load is properly secured and within legal limits
Total weight, as long as all safety systems have been tested and verified
Weight of load, when operating a vehicle with a GVWR over 26,001 pounds
Weight of empty vehicle
correct_answer: Weight of empty vehicle
explanation: Tare weight is the weight of the vehicle when it is empty.

question: What is 'payload'?
options: Fuel weight, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Weight of cargo being transported
Total weight, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Empty weight, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: Weight of cargo being transported
explanation: Payload is the weight of the cargo being transported.

question: What is 'coupling'?
options: Loading, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Parking, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Fueling, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Connecting a tractor to a trailer
correct_answer: Connecting a tractor to a trailer
explanation: Coupling is the process of connecting a tractor to a trailer.

question: What is 'uncoupling'?
options: Stopping, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Coupling, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Driving, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Disconnecting a tractor from a trailer
correct_answer: Disconnecting a tractor from a trailer
explanation: Uncoupling is the process of disconnecting a tractor from a trailer.

question: What is the 'fifth wheel'?
options: Trailer wheel, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Coupling device on the tractor
Spare tire, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Steering wheel, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: Coupling device on the tractor
explanation: The fifth wheel is the coupling device mounted on the rear of the tractor that connects to the trailer kingpin.

question: What is the 'kingpin'?
options: Pin on tractor, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Locking pin, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Hitch pin, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Pin on trailer that connects to fifth wheel
correct_answer: Pin on trailer that connects to fifth wheel
explanation: The kingpin is the pin under the front of the trailer that locks into the tractor's fifth wheel.

question: What is the 'apron'?
options: Driver's clothes, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Metal plate on bottom front of trailer
Fifth wheel cover, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Road surface, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: Metal plate on bottom front of trailer
explanation: The apron is the metal plate on the bottom front of the trailer that rests on the fifth wheel.

question: What should happen when coupling?
options: Doesn't matter, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Large gap, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Small gap, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
No gap between apron and fifth wheel
correct_answer: No gap between apron and fifth wheel
explanation: When coupled, there should be no gap between the trailer apron and the fifth wheel. A gap indicates a high hook.

question: What is 'landing gear'?
options: Tractor wheels, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Airplane wheels, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Supports that hold up trailer when uncoupled
Ramps, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: Supports that hold up trailer when uncoupled
explanation: Landing gear (dollies) are the retractable supports that hold up the front of the trailer when it is uncoupled from the tractor.

question: What is the most important reason for doing a vehicle inspection?
options: To save fuel, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Because the boss says so, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
To keep the truck clean, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Safety for yourself and other road users
correct_answer: Safety for yourself and other road users
explanation: Safety is the most important reason you inspect your vehicle, safety for yourself and for other road users.

question: What things should you check during a trip?
options: Check your phone, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Rest in the sleeper berth until the situation resolves itself
Watch gauges, check mirrors, use senses (look, listen, smell, feel)
Read a book, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: Watch gauges, check mirrors, use senses (look, listen, smell, feel)
explanation: During a trip, you should watch gauges, check mirrors, and use your senses to check for problems (look, listen, smell, feel).

question: Name some key steering system parts.
options: Drive shaft, differential, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures, provided all air pressure gauges read within normal operating range
Leaf spring, shock absorber, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles, provided the load is properly secured and within legal limits
Brake drum, slack adjuster, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment, provided all air pressure gauges read within normal operating range
Steering wheel, tie rod, steering shaft, power steering fluid reservoir, steering arm, spindle, steering knuckle, drag link, pitman arm, gear box
correct_answer: Steering wheel, tie rod, steering shaft, power steering fluid reservoir, steering arm, spindle, steering knuckle, drag link, pitman arm, gear box
explanation: Key steering parts include the steering wheel, tie rod, shaft, fluid reservoir, arm, spindle, knuckle, drag link, pitman arm, and gear box.

question: Name some suspension system defects.
options: Broken windows, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures, only when driving under a special state or federal permit
Spring hangers that allow movement of axle from proper position, cracked or broken spring hangers, missing or broken leaves
Leaking oil, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment, provided all air pressure gauges read within normal operating range
Flat tires, after confirming all safety checks have been completed, following established DOT pre-trip inspection requirements
correct_answer: Spring hangers that allow movement of axle from proper position, cracked or broken spring hangers, missing or broken leaves
explanation: Suspension defects include broken spring hangers, shifted axles, missing/broken leaves, leaking shocks, and air system leaks.

question: What three kinds of emergency equipment must you have?
options: Radio, phone, charger, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
First aid kit, blanket, water, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles, as long as all safety systems have been tested and verified
Fire extinguisher, spare electrical fuses (unless equipped with circuit breakers), and warning devices (triangles/flares)
Tool kit, jack, spare tire, after confirming all safety checks have been completed, when hauling hazardous materials over a designated route
correct_answer: Fire extinguisher, spare electrical fuses (unless equipped with circuit breakers), and warning devices (triangles/flares)
explanation: You must have a fire extinguisher, spare electrical fuses (if needed), and warning devices for parked vehicles.

question: What is the minimum tread depth for front tires?
options: 1/2 inch
2/32 inch
4/32 inch
1/32 inch
correct_answer: 4/32 inch
explanation: Front tires must have a minimum tread depth of 4/32 inch in every major groove.

question: What is the minimum tread depth for other tires?
options: 4/32 inch
1/4 inch
1/32 inch
2/32 inch
correct_answer: 2/32 inch
explanation: Tires other than front tires must have a minimum tread depth of 2/32 inch.

question: Name some things you should check on the front of your vehicle during the walk-around inspection.
options: Condition of front axle, steering system, windshield, lights, and reflectors
Trailer doors, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Rear brakes, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Exhaust system, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: Condition of front axle, steering system, windshield, lights, and reflectors
explanation: Check the front axle, steering system, windshield, lights, reflectors, and turn signals.

question: What should wheel bearing seals be checked for?
options: Rust
Size
Leaking
Paint color
correct_answer: Leaking
explanation: Check wheel bearing seals for leaks. Leaking seals can cause wheel failure or fires.

question: How many red reflective triangles should you carry?
options: One
Three
Two
Four
correct_answer: Three
explanation: You must carry three red reflective triangles (or equivalent warning devices).

question: How do you test hydraulic brakes for leaks?
options: Pump the brake pedal three times, then apply firm pressure to the pedal and hold for five seconds
Drive fast and stop, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles, only after completing a thorough pre-trip safety inspection
Look under the truck, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment, as long as all safety systems have been tested and verified
Listen for air, after confirming all safety checks have been completed, as long as all safety systems have been tested and verified
correct_answer: Pump the brake pedal three times, then apply firm pressure to the pedal and hold for five seconds
explanation: Pump the pedal 3 times, then hold firm pressure for 5 seconds. The pedal should not move. If it does, there may be a leak.

question: Why put the starter switch key in your pocket during the pre-trip inspection?
options: It's the law, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
So you don't lose it, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
To keep it warm, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
To prevent someone else from moving the truck while you are under it
correct_answer: To prevent someone else from moving the truck while you are under it
explanation: Keep the key in your pocket for safety so no one can start or move the vehicle while you are inspecting it.

question: Why should you back toward the driver's side?
options: No reason, following established DOT pre-trip inspection requirements
It's harder, unless directed otherwise by law enforcement or a flag person
It's illegal to back to the right
So you can see better
correct_answer: So you can see better
explanation: Backing toward the driver's side allows you to watch the rear of your vehicle out the side window, providing better visibility.

question: If stopped on a hill, how can you start moving without rolling back?
options: Roll back slightly, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Release brake quickly, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Rev the engine high, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Partly engage the clutch before taking your foot off the brake
correct_answer: Partly engage the clutch before taking your foot off the brake
explanation: Partly engage the clutch to the friction point and hold it before releasing the brake to prevent rolling back.

question: When backing, why is it important to use a helper?
options: To hold the door, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
It's required by law, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
To talk to, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Because there are blind spots you can't see
correct_answer: Because there are blind spots you can't see
explanation: There are blind spots you cannot see. A helper can guide you and warn of hazards.

question: What is the most important hand signal that you and the helper should agree on?
options: Stop
Go
Right
Left
correct_answer: Stop
explanation: The most important signal is 'Stop'. Agree on it before starting.

question: What are the two special conditions where you should downshift?
options: Before parking and before backing, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Before passing and before merging, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Before starting down a hill and before entering a curve
Before stopping and before turning, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: Before starting down a hill and before entering a curve
explanation: Downshift before starting down a hill to use engine braking, and before entering a curve to maintain control.

question: When should you downshift automatic transmissions?
options: Never, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
When going up a hill, as specified in the state commercial driver licensing manual
Only when stopped, provided all air pressure gauges read within normal operating range
Before going down a steep grade
correct_answer: Before going down a steep grade
explanation: Select a low range or gear before going down a steep grade to utilize engine braking.

question: Retarders keep you from skidding when the road is slippery. True or False?
options: False
True
correct_answer: False
explanation: False. Retarders can cause the drive wheels to skid on slippery surfaces. Turn them off when the road is wet, icy, or snowy.

question: What are the two ways to know when to shift?
options: Guess and check, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Use engine speed (RPM) and road speed (mph)
Ask a passenger, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Listen to engine and feel vibration
correct_answer: Use engine speed (RPM) and road speed (mph)
explanation: Use engine speed (RPM) and road speed (mph) to determine shift points. Check your manual for specific speeds.

question: How far ahead does the manual say you should look?
options: 12 to 15 seconds
As far as you can see
5 to 10 seconds
1 to 2 seconds
correct_answer: 12 to 15 seconds
explanation: Look 12 to 15 seconds ahead. At highway speeds, this is about a quarter of a mile.

question: What are two main things to look for ahead?
options: Signs and billboards
Police and weigh stations
Rest areas and food
Traffic and road conditions
correct_answer: Traffic and road conditions
explanation: Look for traffic (vehicles entering, slowing, turning) and road conditions (hills, curves, signals, pavement issues).

question: What's your most important way to see the sides and rear of your vehicle?
options: Mirrors
Camera
Turning your head
Helper
correct_answer: Mirrors
explanation: Mirrors are your most important tool for seeing the sides and rear. Check them regularly.

question: What does 'communicating' mean in safe driving?
options: Using hand signals, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Signaling your intentions to other drivers
Yelling, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Talking on CB, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: Signaling your intentions to other drivers
explanation: Communicating means signaling your intentions (turns, lane changes, slowing down) to other drivers using lights and horn.

question: Where should you place reflectors when stopped on a divided highway?
options: 10 feet, 100 feet, and 200 feet toward the approaching traffic
100 feet front, 10 feet rear, 100 feet rear, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
On the roof, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Beside the truck, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: 10 feet, 100 feet, and 200 feet toward the approaching traffic
explanation: On a divided highway, place warning devices 10 feet, 100 feet, and 200 feet behind the vehicle.

question: What three things add up to total stopping distance?
options: Perception Distance + Reaction Distance + Braking Distance
Thinking Distance + Braking Distance, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Reaction Distance + Skidding Distance, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Seeing Distance + Stopping Distance, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: Perception Distance + Reaction Distance + Braking Distance
explanation: Total Stopping Distance = Perception Distance + Reaction Distance + Braking Distance.

question: If you go twice as fast, will your stopping distance increase by two or four times?
options: Four times
Two times
Eight times
Three times
correct_answer: Four times
explanation: When speed doubles, braking distance increases by four times.

question: Empty trucks have the best braking. True or False?
options: False
True
correct_answer: False
explanation: False. Empty trucks require greater stopping distances because they have less traction and the brakes may lock up easier.

question: What is hydroplaning?
options: When tires lose contact with the road and ride on water
Flying, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Washing the truck, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Driving a boat, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: When tires lose contact with the road and ride on water
explanation: Hydroplaning is when water builds up under the tires, lifting them off the road surface.

question: What is 'black ice'?
options: Asphalt, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures, when operating a vehicle with a GVWR over 26,001 pounds
Oil, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles, according to the federal motor carrier safety regulations
A thin layer of ice clear enough that you can see the road underneath it
Dirty ice, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: A thin layer of ice clear enough that you can see the road underneath it
explanation: Black ice is thin, clear ice that makes the road look wet. It is very slippery.

question: How do you find out how many seconds of following distance space you have?
options: Use a stopwatch, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures, following established DOT pre-trip inspection requirements
Wait until the vehicle ahead passes a shadow or landmark, then count the seconds until you reach it
Estimate the weight distribution based on the load appearance, only after completing a thorough pre-trip safety inspection
Ask your helper, after confirming all safety checks have been completed, once the vehicle has been inspected and cleared for operation
correct_answer: Wait until the vehicle ahead passes a shadow or landmark, then count the seconds until you reach it
explanation: Pick a landmark. When the vehicle ahead passes it, count seconds until you reach it.

question: If you are driving a 30-foot vehicle at 55 mph, how many seconds of following distance should you allow?
options: 6 seconds
4 seconds
5 seconds
3 seconds
correct_answer: 4 seconds
explanation: 1 second for every 10 feet of vehicle length (3 seconds) + 1 second for speed over 40 mph = 4 seconds.

question: You should decrease your following distance if somebody is following you too closely. True or False?
options: True
False
correct_answer: False
explanation: False. Increase your following distance. This opens up room for the tailgater to pass you.

question: If you swing wide to the left before turning right, another driver may try to pass you on the right. True or False?
options: True
False
correct_answer: True
explanation: True. Keep the rear of your vehicle close to the curb to prevent drivers from passing on the right.

question: What is a hazard?
options: A truck, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
A law, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
A sign, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Any road condition or other road user that is a possible danger
correct_answer: Any road condition or other road user that is a possible danger
explanation: A hazard is any road condition or other road user (driver, bicyclist, pedestrian) that is a possible danger.

question: Why make emergency plans when you see a hazard?
options: To be polite, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
To save fuel, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
It's required, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
So you will have more time to act if the hazard becomes an emergency
correct_answer: So you will have more time to act if the hazard becomes an emergency
explanation: Planning ahead gives you more time to act safely if the hazard turns into a real emergency.

question: What are some tips to follow so you won't become a distracted driver?
options: Watch movies, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures, as specified in the state commercial driver licensing manual
Read while driving, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles, according to the federal motor carrier safety regulations
Text while driving, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Pre-program radio stations, review maps before driving, don't eat or drink while driving
correct_answer: Pre-program radio stations, review maps before driving, don't eat or drink while driving
explanation: Prepare before you drive. Adjust mirrors, radio, and review route. Don't eat, drink, or use phones while driving.

question: How do you use in-vehicle communications equipment cautiously?
options: Look at the screen, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Position it within reach, keep conversations short, hang up in traffic
Hold the phone, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Talk constantly, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: Position it within reach, keep conversations short, hang up in traffic
explanation: Keep equipment within reach. Keep conversations short. Hang up in heavy traffic or hazardous conditions.

question: How do you recognize a distracted driver?
options: Drifting lanes, inconsistent speed, preoccupied with objects
Maintaining speed, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Driving straight, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Using signals, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: Drifting lanes, inconsistent speed, preoccupied with objects
explanation: Distracted drivers often drift, change speed for no reason, or appear to be looking at something else.

question: What is the difference between aggressive driving and road rage?
options: Aggressive driving is legal, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Aggressive driving is selfish/pushy; road rage is intent to harm
Road rage is safer, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
They are the same, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: Aggressive driving is selfish/pushy; road rage is intent to harm
explanation: Aggressive driving is operating in a bold/pushy manner. Road rage is angry behavior with intent to do physical harm.

question: What should you do when confronted with an aggressive driver?
options: Challenge them, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Make every attempt to get out of their way
Block them, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Yell at them, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: Make every attempt to get out of their way
explanation: Avoid conflict. Get out of their way safely. Do not make eye contact. Ignore gestures.

question: What are some things you can do to reduce your stress before and while driving?
options: Drive faster, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures, as specified in the state commercial driver licensing manual
Yell at traffic, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles, once the vehicle has been inspected and cleared for operation
Listen to easy music, give yourself plenty of time, accept that you can't control traffic
Drink coffee, after confirming all safety checks have been completed, provided the load is properly secured and within legal limits
correct_answer: Listen to easy music, give yourself plenty of time, accept that you can't control traffic
explanation: Plan ahead, be realistic about travel time, listen to calming music, and accept that delays happen.

question: You should use low beams whenever you can. True or False?
options: True
False
correct_answer: False
explanation: False. Use high beams when it is safe and legal to do so for better visibility.

question: What should you do before you drive if you are drowsy?
options: Take a cold shower
Exercise
Sleep
Drink coffee
correct_answer: Sleep
explanation: Sleep is the only thing that can overcome fatigue. If you are drowsy, get some sleep before driving.

question: What effects can wet brakes cause?
options: Brakes squeak, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Brakes work better, provided all air pressure gauges read within normal operating range
Brakes can grab or become weak
No effect, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: Brakes can grab or become weak
explanation: Water in the brakes can cause them to be weak, apply unevenly, or grab. This can cause jackknifing.

question: How can you avoid wet brake problems?
options: Don't brake, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Accelerate quickly to build up system pressure before stopping
Stop completely, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Slow down, place transmission in low gear, gently apply brakes
correct_answer: Slow down, place transmission in low gear, gently apply brakes
explanation: Slow down, use a low gear, and lightly apply brakes to heat them up and dry them out.

question: You should let air out of hot tires so the pressure goes back to normal. True or False?
options: False
True
correct_answer: False
explanation: False. Do not let air out. When the tires cool, the pressure will be too low.

question: You can safely remove the radiator cap as long as the engine isn't overheated. True or False?
options: False
True
correct_answer: False
explanation: False. Never remove the radiator cap until the system has cooled. Steam and boiling water can burn you.

question: What factors determine your selection of a 'safe' speed when going down a long, steep downgrade?
options: Speed limit only, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Total weight, length of grade, steepness of grade, road conditions, weather
Time of day, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Traffic only, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: Total weight, length of grade, steepness of grade, road conditions, weather
explanation: Consider total weight, cargo, length and steepness of the grade, road conditions, and weather.

question: Why should you be in the proper gear before starting down a hill?
options: You might not be able to shift into a lower gear once you are going fast
To go faster, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
It's the law, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
To save fuel, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: You might not be able to shift into a lower gear once you are going fast
explanation: Once speed increases, you may not be able to shift into a lower gear. You could lose all engine braking.

question: Describe the proper braking technique when going down a long, steep downgrade.
options: Don't use brakes, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Pump the brakes, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Ride the brakes, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Apply brakes to 5 mph below safe speed, release, repeat
correct_answer: Apply brakes to 5 mph below safe speed, release, repeat
explanation: Apply brakes firmly to slow 5 mph below safe speed, release to let them cool, repeat when speed increases.

question: What type of vehicles can get stuck on a railroad-highway crossing?
options: Box trucks, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Flatbeds, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Low slung units (lowboys, car carriers, moving vans)
Tankers, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: Low slung units (lowboys, car carriers, moving vans)
explanation: Low slung units like lowboys, car carriers, and moving vans can get hung up on raised crossings.

question: How long does it take for a typical tractor-trailer unit to clear a double track?
options: 10 seconds, when hauling hazardous materials over a designated route
More than 15 seconds
1 minute, when hauling hazardous materials over a designated route
5 seconds, unless directed otherwise by law enforcement or a flag person
correct_answer: More than 15 seconds
explanation: It takes a typical tractor-trailer at least 14 seconds to clear a single track and more than 15 seconds to clear a double track.

question: Stopping is not always the safest thing to do in an emergency. True or False?
options: True
False
correct_answer: True
explanation: True. Sometimes you can steer away from a hazard more quickly than you can stop.

question: What are some advantages of going right instead of left around an obstacle?
options: You avoid driving into oncoming traffic
It's easier, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
It's faster, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
No advantage, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: You avoid driving into oncoming traffic
explanation: Going right avoids forcing you into the path of oncoming traffic. Also, the shoulder is usually clear.

question: What is an 'escape ramp'?
options: A rest area, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
An exit, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
A bed of loose, soft material to stop a runaway vehicle
A parking spot, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: A bed of loose, soft material to stop a runaway vehicle
explanation: Escape ramps are designed to stop runaway vehicles safely using soft gravel or an uphill grade.

question: If a tire blows out, you should put the brakes on hard to stop quickly. True or False?
options: False
True
correct_answer: False
explanation: False. Braking hard can cause loss of control. Hold the steering wheel firmly and stay off the brake until you have slowed down.

question: How do you know if your vehicle has antilock brakes?
options: Estimate the weight distribution based on the load appearance
Ask the mechanic, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Check the tires, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Look for the yellow ABS malfunction lamp on the instrument panel
correct_answer: Look for the yellow ABS malfunction lamp on the instrument panel
explanation: Vehicles with ABS have a yellow malfunction lamp on the instrument panel. Trailers have a lamp on the left side.

question: What is the proper braking technique when driving a vehicle with antilock brakes?
options: Brake as you always have
Don't brake, unless directed otherwise by law enforcement or a flag person
Pump the brakes, according to the federal motor carrier safety regulations
Slam the brakes, only after completing a thorough pre-trip safety inspection
correct_answer: Brake as you always have
explanation: Use normal braking techniques. ABS simply helps prevent wheel lockup.

question: How do antilock brakes help you?
options: They stop faster, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
They steer for you, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
They save fuel, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
They help you keep your vehicle under control during hard braking
correct_answer: They help you keep your vehicle under control during hard braking
explanation: ABS helps prevent wheel lockup, allowing you to maintain steering control during hard braking.

question: What are some things to do at an accident scene to prevent another accident?
options: Leave quickly, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Take photos, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Protect the area, notify authorities, care for the injured
Argue, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: Protect the area, notify authorities, care for the injured
explanation: Protect the area (warning devices), notify authorities, and care for the injured.

question: Name two causes of tire fires.
options: New tires and rain, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Old tires and snow, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Under-inflated tires and duals that touch
Clean tires and sun, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: Under-inflated tires and duals that touch
explanation: Under-inflation causes heat buildup. Touching duals cause friction and heat.

question: What kinds of fires is a B:C extinguisher not good for?
options: Grease, according to standard pre-trip inspection procedures
Wood, paper, and ordinary combustibles (Class A)
Gasoline, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
Electrical, after confirming all safety checks have been completed
correct_answer: Wood, paper, and ordinary combustibles (Class A)
explanation: B:C extinguishers are for liquids and electrical fires. They are not effective on Class A fires (wood, paper).

question: When using your extinguisher, should you get as close as possible to the fire?
options: Yes, get right in it, unless the vehicle is equipped with an automatic safety override
Stand on it, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
Doesn't matter, only if the vehicle is equipped with the proper safety equipment
No, stay as far away as possible
correct_answer: No, stay as far away as possible
explanation: Stay as far away as possible while still being able to hit the fire with the agent.

question: Name some causes of vehicle fires.
options: Spilled fuel, short circuits, under-inflated tires, flammable cargo
Snow, as required by FMCSA regulations for commercial vehicles
